blocks:
Ayahs

الٓمٓ (١)
Alif, Lam, Meem. (1)
أَحَسِبَ ٱلنَّاسُ أَن يُتْرَكُوٓا۟ أَن يَقُولُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَهُمْ لَا يُفْتَنُونَ (٢)
Do the people suppose that they will be let off because they say, ‘We have faith,’ and they will not be tested? (2)
وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۖ فَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا۟ وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٣)
Certainly We tested those who were before them. So Allah shall surely ascertain those who are truthful, and He shall surely ascertain the liars. (3)
أَمْ حَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ أَن يَسْبِقُونَا ۚ سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ (٤)
Do those who commit misdeeds suppose that they can outmaneuver Us? Evil is the judgement that they make. (4)
مَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ لِقَآءَ ٱللَّهِ فَإِنَّ أَجَلَ ٱللَّهِ لَـَٔاتٍ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٥)
Whoever expects to encounter Allah [should know that] Allah’s [appointed] time will indeed come, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (5)
وَمَن جَـٰهَدَ فَإِنَّمَا يُجَـٰهِدُ لِنَفْسِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَغَنِىٌّ عَنِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦)
Whoever strives, strives only for his own sake. Indeed Allah has no need of the creatures. (6)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَنُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَحْسَنَ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٧)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We will absolve them of their misdeeds and We will surely reward them by the best of what they used to do. (7)
وَوَصَّيْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ حُسْنًا ۖ وَإِن جَـٰهَدَاكَ لِتُشْرِكَ بِى مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ فَلَا تُطِعْهُمَآ ۚ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٨)
We have enjoined man to be good to his parents. But if they urge you to ascribe to Me as partner that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them. To Me will be your return, whereat I will inform you concerning what you used to do. (8)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَنُدْخِلَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٩)
Those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We will surely admit them among the righteous. (9)
وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ فَإِذَآ أُوذِىَ فِى ٱللَّهِ جَعَلَ فِتْنَةَ ٱلنَّاسِ كَعَذَابِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَئِن جَآءَ نَصْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكَ لَيَقُولُنَّ إِنَّا كُنَّا مَعَكُمْ ۚ أَوَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَعْلَمَ بِمَا فِى صُدُورِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٠)
Among the people there are those who say, ‘We have faith in Allah,’ but if such a one is tormented in Allah’s cause, he takes persecution by the people for Allah’s punishment. Yet if there comes any help from your Lord, they will say, ‘We were indeed with you.’ Does not Allah know best what is in the breasts of the creatures? (10)
وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ (١١)
Allah shall surely ascertain those who have faith, and He shall surely ascertain the hypocrites. (11)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ سَبِيلَنَا وَلْنَحْمِلْ خَطَـٰيَـٰكُمْ وَمَا هُم بِحَـٰمِلِينَ مِنْ خَطَـٰيَـٰهُم مِّن شَىْءٍ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (١٢)
The faithless say to the faithful, ‘Follow our way and we will bear [responsibility for] your iniquities.’ They will not bear anything of their iniquities. They are indeed liars. (12)
وَلَيَحْمِلُنَّ أَثْقَالَهُمْ وَأَثْقَالًا مَّعَ أَثْقَالِهِمْ ۖ وَلَيُسْـَٔلُنَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ عَمَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (١٣)
But they will carry their own burdens and other burdens along with their own burdens, and they will surely be questioned on the Day of Resurrection concerning that which they used to fabricate. (13)

(Commentary)

It appears from the context (السياق) of the verses of this Surah, especially the opening verses, that some of those who believed in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in Mecca before the Migration (الهجرة) turned back from him out of fear of the tribulation (الفتنة) that threatened them from the polytheists (المشركون). Indeed, the polytheists used to call them to return to their creed (ملتهم) and guaranteed that they would carry their sins (خطاياهم) if they followed their path; if they refused, they would persecute and torture them to force them back to their creed.

This is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّبِعُوا سَبِيلَنا وَلْنَحْمِلْ خَطاياكُمْ — "The faithless say to the faithful, ‘Follow our way and let us bear your sins’" (29:12), and His saying: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَقُولُ آمَنَّا بِاللهِ فَإِذا أُوذِيَ فِي اللهِ جَعَلَ فِتْنَةَ النَّاسِ كَعَذابِ اللهِ — "Among the people are those who say, ‘We believe in Allah,’ but if they suffer in [the way of] Allah, they regard the people’s persecution as Allah’s punishment" (29:10).

Among those who turned back from their faith were those whose reversal was due to the exertion (مجاهدة) of their parents and their persistence in demanding apostasy (الارتداد), as was the case with some children of the polytheists. This is sensed from His saying, the Exalted: وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسانَ بِوالِدَيْهِ حُسْناً وَإِنْ جاهَداكَ لِتُشْرِكَ بِي ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ فَلا تُطِعْهُما — "We have enjoined man to be good to his parents. But if they strive with you to make you ascribe to Me as partner that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them" (29:8). The Surah was revealed regarding such people.

The objective of the Surah, as gathered from its beginning, its end, and its prevailing context, is that what Allah, the Glorified, desires from faith (الإيمان) is not merely their saying: "We believe in Allah" (آمَنَّا بِاللهِ). Rather, it is the reality of faith (حقيقة الإيمان) — the true inner conviction that remains unshaken by the storms of tribulation and unchanged by the vicissitudes of time. This reality is only solidified and established through the recurrence of tribulations and the accumulation of trials (المحن). Thus, people are not left alone merely because they say, "We believe in Allah," without being tried (يفتنوا) and tested (يمتحنوا), so that the reality of faith or the stain of disbelief (الكفر) within their souls may be manifested; so that Allah may surely know those who are truthful and know the liars.

Tribulation (الفتنة) and trial (المحنة) are a divine sunnah (سنة إلهية) — an established law of Allah's governance — from which there is no deviation. it applies to the present people just as it applied to past nations (الأمم الماضين), such as the people of Noah, ‘Ad, Thamud, and the people of Abraham, Lot, Shu‘ayb, and Moses. Among them, those who remained steadfast did so, and those who perished did so. Allah did not wrong them, but they used to wrong themselves.

Therefore, whoever says, "I believe in Allah," must be patient (يصبر) in his faith and worship Allah alone. If it becomes impossible for him to perform the duties of religion (الدين), he must migrate to a land where he can do so, for Allah’s earth is spacious. He should not fear the hardship of livelihood, for provision (الرزق) is upon Allah; how many a creature carries not its own provision, yet Allah provides for it and for him.

As for the polytheists who persecute the believers for no crime they committed except saying, "Our Lord is Allah," they should not think they can frustrate Allah or outrun Him. Their persecution, harm, and torture of the believers are but a trial for them and for the believers, not outside of Allah’s knowledge and decree (تقديره). It is a tribulation (فتنة) recorded against them; if He wills, He will seize them for its evil consequence (وبالها) in this world, and if He wills, He will delay them until the Day they return to Him, and they shall have no escape.

As for the arguments they fabricated and the false speech they relied upon, it is futile and refuted, and the proof (الحجة) stands complete against them.

This is the summary of the Surah's objective. This necessitates that the entire Surah is Meccan (مكية). The claim of some that it is Medinan (مدنية) in its entirety, or mostly, or partially—as will be discussed in the following traditional report—is incorrect. The contents of the verses of the Surah are only suited to the time of hardship and severity before the Migration.

His saying, the Exalted: الم أَحَسِبَ النَّاسُ أَنْ يُتْرَكُوا أَنْ يَقُولُوا آمَنَّا وَهُمْ لا يُفْتَنُونَ — "Alif, Lam, Mim. Do the people suppose that they will be let off because they say, ‘We believe,’ and they will not be tested?" (29:1-2). الحسبان (supposition) means conjecture (الظن). The phrase "that they will be let off" (أَنْ يُتْرَكُوا) stands in place of its two objects. His saying "because they say" (أَنْ يَقُولُوا) implies a causal preposition (باء السببية). الفتنة (tribulation) means trial (الامتحان) — the testing of a person's character and faith — and is sometimes applied to calamity and punishment. The first meaning is more suited to the context. The interrogative is for denial (الإنكار).

The meaning is: Do people think they will be left alone, their state unexamined, and not be tested by that which manifests their truthfulness or falsehood in their claim to faith, merely by their saying, "We believe"?

It is also said the meaning is: Do people think they will be left alone and not be afflicted by any trial or calamity because they said, "We believe," as if they have a special honor (كرامة) with Allah due to faith by which they are saved from every unpleasantness that afflicts a human during his life? This is not without some distance from the context of the verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ اللهُ الَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ الْكاذِبِينَ — "Certainly We tested those who were before them. So Allah shall surely ascertain those who are truthful, and He shall surely ascertain the liars" (29:3). The two lams are for an oath (القسم). His saying "Certainly We tested those who were before them" (وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ) is a circumstantial clause (حال) for "the people" in His saying "Do the people suppose," or for the plural pronoun in "they will not be tested." According to the first, the denial and rebuke are directed at their thought that they will not be tested despite the prevalence of the divine sunnah of tribulation and trial. According to the second, it is directed at their thought of inconsistency in Allah's action, where He tests some and not others. Perhaps the first view is more suited to the context.

It is apparent that the meaning of "Certainly We tested those who were before them" is that tribulation and trial are a prevailing sunnah for Us; it applied to those before them and it applies to them, and you will never find any change in the sunnah of Allah.

His saying: "So Allah shall surely ascertain those who are truthful" (فَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ اللهُ الَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا) etc., is an explanation of the reason (تعليل) for what preceded. What is meant by His knowledge (علمه) of the truthful and the liars is the manifestation of the effects of their truthfulness and falsehood in the realm of action through tribulation and trial, which is necessary for the reality of faith to be established in their hearts or its absence. For the felicity (السعادة) that results from the faith called for, as well as the reward (الثواب), only results from the reality of faith (حقيقة الإيمان) which has manifest effects such as patience (الصبر) during hardships, patience in obedience to Allah, and patience against disobeying Allah—not from a mere claim to faith.

It is possible that what is meant by knowledge is His active knowledge (علمه الفعلي), which is the external reality itself, for external matters are themselves among the levels of His knowledge. As for His essential knowledge (علمه الذاتي), it does not depend on trial at all.

The meaning is: Do they suppose they will be left alone and not be tested merely for claiming and manifesting faith, while tribulation is Our sunnah and has applied to those before them? It is necessary that the truthful be distinguished from the liars by the manifestation of the effects of the former's truthfulness and the latter's falsehood, which is necessary for the establishment of faith in the hearts of the former and the removal of its false image from the hearts of the latter.

The shift (الالتفات) in His saying "So Allah shall surely ascertain" (فَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ اللهُ) to the Name of Majesty (اسم الجلالة) is said to be for inspiring awe (التهويل) and cultivating reverence (المهابة). It appears that in such contexts, it serves to provide a type of reasoning (تعليل). This is because the call to faith, guidance toward it, and reward for it all return to the One named "Allah," from Whom everything begins, by Whom everything subsists, and to Whom everything returns in its reality. Thus, it is necessary that the reality of faith be distinguished before Him from its empty claim, moving from a state of ambiguity to a state of clarity. Therefore, He turned from saying "so We shall surely know" to "so Allah shall surely ascertain."

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ يَسْبِقُونا ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ — "Do those who commit misdeeds suppose that they can outmaneuver Us? Evil is the judgment that they make" (29:4). The word am (أَمْ) is disjunctive (منقطعة). What is meant by "those who commit misdeeds" (الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ) are the polytheists who were persecuting the believers and barring them from the way of Allah, just as "the people" in "Do the people suppose" refers to those who said, "We believe," and were prone to turning back from faith out of fear of tribulation and torture.

What is meant by "that they can outmaneuver Us" (أَنْ يَسْبِقُونا) is overcoming and frustrating [Allah's plan] through the persecution of believers and barring them from the way of Allah—as suggested by the context.

His saying: "Evil is the judgment that they make" (ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ) is a refutation of their thought that they outmaneuver Allah with the plots they devise of tribulation and barring [from the path]. For that very act is a tribulation from Allah for themselves and a barring of themselves from the path of felicity; and the evil plot only besets its authors.

It is said the verse is a rebuke to the sinners among the believers, and they are the ones meant by "those who commit misdeeds," and "misdeeds" refers to the sins they commit other than polytheism (الشرك). You are aware that the context does not support this.

It is also said that "committing misdeeds" is more general than polytheism and the commission of other sins; thus the verse is general and there is no reason to restrict it specifically to polytheism or specifically to other sins excluding polytheism.

The response is that considering the verse in terms of its occurrence in a specific context is one thing, and considering it independently in itself is another. What the first consideration necessitates—which is the primary one regarding the objective of the Surah—is the meaning we presented. As for the second consideration, it necessitates generality, and there is no harm in that on that assumption.

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ اللهِ فَإِنَّ أَجَلَ اللهِ لَآتٍ وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ — "Whoever expects to encounter Allah [should know that] Allah’s appointed time will surely come, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing" (29:5) until the end of the three verses. When He, the Glorified, rebuked the people for their belittling of the matter of faith and their turning back from it due to any tribulation or harm from the polytheists, and rebuked the polytheists for their persecution and harm of the believers and their barring from the way of Allah—intending to extinguish the light of Allah and frustrate Him in what He willed—and corrected both groups in what they supposed;

He returned to explaining the Truth from which there is no deviation and the Duty from which there is no escape. He explained in these three verses that whoever believes in Allah in expectation of returning to Him and encountering Him, let him know that it is inevitably coming, and that Allah is All-hearing of his words, All-knowing of his states and deeds. So let him take heed and believe with the true faith that no tribulation or harm can turn him away from, and let him strive (ليجاهد) for Allah with the striving that is His due. Let him know that the one who benefits from his striving is himself, and Allah, the Glorified, has no need for his faith nor for any of the worlds. Let him know that if he believes and performs righteous deeds (الصالحات), Allah will surely absolve him of his misdeeds and reward him for the best of his deeds. These latter two pieces of knowledge reinforce the first knowledge and necessitate his adherence to faith and his patience over tribulations and trials for the sake of Allah.

So His saying: "Whoever expects to encounter Allah" (مَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ اللهِ) is a return to explaining the state of the one who says, "I believe." For he only believes—if he is somewhat truthful—due to his expectation of returning to Allah, the Glorified, on the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة); for were it not for the Return (المعاد), religion would be futile in its essence. Thus, what is meant by "Whoever expects to encounter Allah" is whoever believes in Allah or whoever says, "I believe in Allah." The sentence is of the type where the cause (السبب) is placed in the position of the effect (المسبب).

What is meant by "encountering Allah" (لقاء الله) is the servant standing in a position where there is no veil between him and his Lord, as is the case on the Day of Resurrection, which is the time for the manifestation of realities. He, the Exalted, said: وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ الْمُبِينُ — "and they will know that Allah is the manifest Truth" (24:25).

It is said that "encountering Allah" means the Resurrection (البعث). It is also said it means reaching the end, such as encountering the Angel of Death, the Reckoning (الحساب), and the Requital (الجزاء). It is also said it means encountering Allah’s requital of reward or punishment. It is also said it means encountering His judgment on the Day of Resurrection. According to some of these views, "expectation" (الرجاء) means fear (الخوف).

These are distant metaphorical views for which there is no necessity, unless they are considered interpretation by the necessary implication of the meaning.

His saying: "Allah’s appointed time will surely come" (فَإِنَّ أَجَلَ اللهِ لَآتٍ). The أجل (appointed time) is the limit at which the time of a debt or similar thing ends; it is also sometimes applied to the entirety of that time, but its most common usage is the first meaning.

"Allah’s appointed time" (أَجَلَ اللهِ) is the limit that Allah has designated for encountering Him, and it is coming without doubt. The statement has been emphasized with great emphasis. The necessity of the inevitable arrival of this appointed time—which is the Day of Resurrection—is that one should not be negligent in its affair, nor belittle the matter of believing in Allah with true faith and being patient upon it during tribulations and trials without turning back or apostatizing. He increased the emphasis of the statement by concluding it with His saying: "and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing" (وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ). Since He, the Exalted, is All-hearing of their words and All-knowing of their states, it is not fitting for a person to say, "I believe in Allah," except from the bottom of the heart and with patience over every tribulation and trial.

From here it becomes clear that the end of the verse: "Allah’s appointed time will surely come" etc., is of the type where the cause is placed in the position of the effect, just as its beginning: "Whoever expects to encounter Allah" was also like that. The original meaning is: Whoever says, "I believe in Allah," let him say it with steadfastness, being patient upon it, and striving for his Lord.

His saying: وَمَنْ جاهَدَ فَإِنَّما يُجاهِدُ لِنَفْسِهِ إِنَّ اللهَ لَغَنِيٌّ عَنِ الْعالَمِينَ — "Whoever strives, strives only for himself. Indeed Allah is independent of all the worlds" (29:6). المجاهدة (striving) and الجهاد (struggle) are intensive forms of juhd (الجهد), meaning the exertion of power. In this is a reminder to them that their striving for Allah—by adhering to faith and being patient over hardships for His sake—is not something whose benefit returns to Allah, the Glorified, such that it would not concern them or be futile for them. Rather, its benefit returns only to themselves because of His independence from all the worlds. Therefore, they must adhere to faith and be patient over hardships for His sake.

So His saying: "Whoever strives, strives only for himself" is a reinforcement of the argument of the previous verse, and His saying: "Indeed Allah is independent of all the worlds" is the reasoning for what preceded.

The shift from the context of the first-person plural to the Name of Majesty in the two verses is similar to the shift mentioned in His saying: "So Allah shall surely ascertain those who are truthful" (29:3).

His saying: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَنُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَحْسَنَ الَّذِي كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We will surely absolve them of their misdeeds and We will surely reward them by the best of what they used to do" (29:7). This is an explanation of the outcome of their true faith accompanied by striving. It clarifies that the benefit of their faith returns to them, not to Allah, the Glorified, and that it is a gift and grace from Allah.

Accordingly, the verse is not without some indication that striving for Allah is faith and righteous deeds; for it is in the sense of substituting His saying in the previous verse "Whoever strives" with His saying in this verse "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds."

The absolution (تكفير) of misdeeds is their forgiveness; the root meaning of kufr (الكفر) is covering (الستر). It is said that the absolution of misdeeds is the changing of their previous disbelief into faith and their previous sins into acts of obedience, but this is not quite correct.

Rewarding them by the best of what they used to do means raising their degree to what suits the best of their deeds, or not scrutinizing their deeds during the Reckoning if they contained aspects of poor quality or meanness. Thus, they are treated in every one of their deeds with the treatment of one who performed the best deed of its kind; so their prayer is counted as the best prayer even if it included some aspects of poor quality, and so on.

His saying, the Exalted: وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسانَ بِوالِدَيْهِ حُسْناً وَإِنْ جاهَداكَ لِتُشْرِكَ بِي ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ فَلا تُطِعْهُما — "We have enjoined man to be good to his parents. But if they strive with you to make you ascribe to Me as partner that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them" (29:8). التوصية (enjoining) is a covenant (العهد), and here it is a command. His saying حُسْناً (goodness) is a verbal noun (مصدر) in the sense of an adjective, standing in place of a deleted absolute object (مفعول مطلق). The estimation is: "We enjoined man regarding his parents with a good enjoining" or "one characterized by goodness," meaning We commanded him to be good to them. This is like His saying: وَقُولُوا لِلنَّاسِ حُسْناً — "and speak kindly to people" (2:83), meaning a good speech or one characterized by goodness. It is also possible that the verbal noun is placed in the position of the adjective for emphasis, like saying "Zayd is justice." Other explanations have also been offered.

His saying: "But if they strive with you to make you ascribe to Me as partner" (وَإِنْ جاهَداكَ لِتُشْرِكَ بِي) etc., is a completion of the enjoining with a direct address to the human being, forbidding him from obeying his parents if they call him to polytheism. The reason for this is that the enjoining is in the sense of a command; so it is as if it were said: "And We said to the human: Be good to your parents, but if they strive with you to make you ascribe to Me as partner that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them."

He did not say "and that he should not obey them if they strive with him to make him ascribe..." because of the clarity and removal of ambiguity in direct address. For this reason, He also said: "to make you ascribe to Me" (لِتُشْرِكَ بِي) with the first-person singular pronoun; so understand this. The meaning of the sentence resolves to: We forbade him from polytheism in obedience to them and We removed all ambiguity from him.

In His saying: "that of which you have no knowledge" (ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ) is an indication of the reason for the prohibition of obedience. For their call to polytheism by worshipping a god besides Allah is a call to ignorance and the worship of that which he has no knowledge of—a fabrication against Allah. Allah has forbidden following other than knowledge; He said: وَلا تَقْفُ ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ — "Do not pursue that of which you have no knowledge" (17:36). On this occasion, He concluded it with His saying: إِلَيَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "To Me will be your return, and I will inform you of what you used to do" (29:8), meaning I will inform you of the meaning of your deeds, including your worship of idols and your ascribing partners to Allah, the Glorified.

The meaning of the verse is: We covenanted with the human regarding his parents a good covenant—and We commanded him: Be good to your parents—but if they exert their effort to make you ascribe partners to Me, do not obey them, for that is following what you have no knowledge of.

In the verse—as previously indicated—is an indirect rebuke to some who had believed and then turned back from their faith due to the exertion of their parents.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَنُدْخِلَنَّهُمْ فِي الصَّالِحِينَ — "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We shall surely admit them among the righteous" (29:9). The meaning of the verse is clear. Its occurrence after the previous verse and in its context indicates a beautiful promise from Him, the Exalted, and a comforting of the soul for those believers afflicted with polytheist parents who strive with them toward polytheism, yet they disobey them and part from them. He, the Glorified, says: If they strive with him toward polytheism and he disobeys them and leaves them, and thus loses them, there is no harm in that; for We shall provide him with better than them and admit him, by his faith and righteous deeds, among the righteous (الصالحين) — the servants who are blessed in Paradise. He, the Exalted, said: يا أَيَّتُهَا النَّفْسُ الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ ارْجِعِي إِلى رَبِّكِ راضِيَةً مَرْضِيَّةً فَادْخُلِي فِي عِبادِي وَادْخُلِي جَنَّتِي — "O soul at peace! Return to your Lord, pleased, pleasing! Then enter among My servants! And enter My paradise!" (89:27-30).

As for the interpretation of "the righteous society in this world," it is distant from the context.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَقُولُ آمَنَّا بِاللهِ فَإِذا أُوذِيَ فِي اللهِ جَعَلَ فِتْنَةَ النَّاسِ كَعَذابِ اللهِ — "Among the people are those who say, ‘We believe in Allah,’ but if they suffer in [the way of] Allah, they regard the people’s persecution as Allah’s punishment" (29:10) until the end of the verse. Since the faith of these people was conditioned upon well-being and safety, and terminated by harm and trial, He did not call it faith in an absolute sense, nor did He say: "Among the people are those who believe in Allah." Rather, He said: "Among the people are those who say, ‘We believe in Allah.’" Thus, the verse is in a way similar to His saying: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَعْبُدُ اللهَ عَلى حَرْفٍ فَإِنْ أَصابَهُ خَيْرٌ اطْمَأَنَّ بِهِ وَإِنْ أَصابَتْهُ فِتْنَةٌ انْقَلَبَ عَلى وَجْهِهِ — "Among the people is he who worships Allah on the verge: if good fortune befalls him, he is content with it; but if an ordeal befalls him, he makes a volte-face" (22:11).

His saying: "if they suffer in [the way of] Allah" (فَإِذا أُوذِيَ فِي اللهِ) means suffered because of faith in Allah, based on the view that "in" (فِي) denotes causality (السببية), as has been said. In this is a subtle rhetorical nuance by making Him, the Exalted—that is, making faith in Allah—the vessel (ظرف) for the suffering and for the one upon whom the suffering falls, to indicate that the suffering is attributed to Him, the Exalted, as the contained is attributed to the container. This aligns with the meaning of causality and purpose. Similar to this is His saying: يا حَسْرَتى عَلى ما فَرَّطْتُ فِي جَنْبِ اللهِ — "Alas for my negligence in the side of Allah!" (39:56) and His saying: وَالَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا — "As for those who strive in Us" (29:69).

It is said: the meaning of suffering "in Allah" is suffering in the way of Allah (في سبيل الله), as if it were based on an estimated deleted possessive noun.

The response is that the rhetorical nuances are different. Suffering "in Allah" is that whose cause is pure faith in Allah, which is their saying: "Our Lord is Allah." Suffering "in the way of Allah" is that whose cause is traversing the path (السبيل) which is the religion. He, the Exalted, said: فَالَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا وَأُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ وَأُوذُوا فِي سَبِيلِي — "So those who migrated and were expelled from their homes, and were persecuted in My way" (3:195). Among the proofs for the difference between the two considerations is His saying at the end of the Surah: "As for those who strive in Us (فِينا), We shall surely guide them to Our ways (سُبُلَنا)," where He made striving "in Allah" a path to being guided to "His ways." If they were of the same meaning, this would not be correct.

His saying: "they regard the people’s persecution as Allah’s punishment" (جَعَلَ فِتْنَةَ النَّاسِ كَعَذابِ اللهِ) means they treat the punishment and harm that afflicts them from people—in the necessity of avoiding it—as the punishment of Allah which must be avoided. So they turn back from faith to polytheism out of fear and anxiety over their tribulation, even though their punishment is slight and terminated by escape or death, and it cannot be compared to the great, eternal punishment of Allah which entails permanent destruction.

His saying: وَلَئِنْ جاءَ نَصْرٌ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لَيَقُولُنَّ إِنَّا كُنَّا مَعَكُمْ — "And if there comes a victory from your Lord, they will surely say, ‘We were indeed with you’" (29:10). That is, if there comes to you from Him, the Exalted, that which contains relief and ease for you after the severity and hardship you were in from the enemies of Allah, these people will surely say, "We were indeed with you," so that we may have a share of it.

"They will surely say" (لَيَقُولُنَ) with the damma on the lam is a plural form. The pronoun refers to "who" (مِنَ) considering the meaning, just as the other singular pronouns refer to it considering the word.

His saying: أَوَلَيْسَ اللهُ بِأَعْلَمَ بِما فِي صُدُورِ الْعالَمِينَ — "Is not Allah most knowing of what is in the breasts of all the worlds?" (29:10). This is an interrogative for denial, containing a refutation of their claim that they are believers, for Allah is most knowing of what is in the breasts, and the hearts of these people do not contain faith.

What is meant by "all the worlds" (الْعَالَمِينَ) are the groups of humans, or the various groups of possessors of intellect, whether human or otherwise, such as jinn and angels. If what was meant were all creatures, whether sentient or otherwise, then "breasts" (الصدور) would mean the inner parts (البواطن), which is distant.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ — "Allah will surely ascertain those who have faith, and He will surely ascertain the hypocrites" (29:11). This is a continuation of the speech in the previous verse. The summary is that Allah, despite that, distinguishes between the believers and the hypocrites (المنافقين) — those who manifest faith while concealing disbelief — through tribulation and trial.

In the verse is an indication that these people are hypocrites. This is because their faith was conditioned upon the absence of tribulation, yet they manifest it absolutely without condition; and tribulation is a prevailing divine sunnah from which there is no deviation.

These two verses have been used to argue that the Surah, or specifically these verses, are Medinan. This is because the verse speaks of hypocrisy (النفاق), and hypocrisy only appeared in Medina after the Migration. As for Mecca before the Migration, Islam had no power there, and the Muslims had nothing but humiliation, insult, severity, and tribulation. Nor did the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) have any might or status in the Arab society of that day, especially among the Quraysh. Thus, there was no motive for anyone to pretend to have faith while intending disbelief.

Furthermore, His saying in the verse: "And if there comes a victory from your Lord, they will surely say, ‘We were indeed with you’" informs of victory (النصر), which is conquest and booty, and that occurred in Medina, not Mecca.

Similar to these two verses is His previous saying: "Whoever strives, strives only for himself," for necessarily, striving (الجهاد) and fighting only occurred in Medina after the Migration.

This [argument] is weak. As for the matter of hypocrisy, that which the verse made a criterion for hypocrisy—namely their saying "We believe in Allah" until they are harmed for Allah's sake and then turn back from their word—was possible to occur in Mecca as elsewhere; this is manifest. In fact, the harm and tribulation mentioned only occurred in Mecca; there was no such tribulation in Medina after the Migration.

As for the matter of victory, victory is not restricted to conquest and booty; it has other instances through which Allah provides relief for His servants. Moreover, the verse does not inform of it in a way that indicates its occurrence. His saying: "but if they suffer in [the way of] Allah, they regard the people’s persecution as Allah’s punishment, and if there comes a victory from your Lord, they will surely say, ‘We were indeed with you’" indicates the occurrence of suffering and tribulation because He used idha (إِذا), which indicates the certainty of occurrence, unlike the coming of victory where He used the conditional in (إِنْ), which indicates the possibility of occurrence without its certainty.

As for His saying, the Exalted: "Whoever strives" (وَمَنْ جاهَدَ) etc., it has become clear from what preceded that what is meant by it is the striving of the soul (جهاد النفس), not fighting the disbelievers. Thus, the truth is that there is no indication in any of the verses that the Surah or part of it is Medinan.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّبِعُوا سَبِيلَنا وَلْنَحْمِلْ خَطاياكُمْ وَما هُمْ بِحامِلِينَ مِنْ خَطاياهُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ — "The faithless say to the faithful, ‘Follow our way and let us bear your sins.’ They will not bear any of their sins. They are indeed liars" (29:12). What is meant by "those who disbelieve" are the polytheists of Mecca who first manifested disbelief toward the true call. By "those who believe" are the believers in it from the first instance. Their saying to them: "Follow our way and let us bear your sins" is a type of enticement and a comforting of their souls, suggesting that if they returned to polytheism and followed their way, there would be no consequence for them in any case. For if there were no sin in that, then so be it; and if there were, they would bear it for them. Therefore, they did not say "and we will bear your sins if there are any," but spoke absolutely without condition.

It is as if they said: Let us assume that your following our way is a sin; we will bear it for you and bear everything that branches from it of sins, or we will bear your sins in general, and among them is this sin.

His saying: "They will not bear any of their sins" (وَما هُمْ بِحامِلِينَ مِنْ خَطاياهُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ) is a refutation of their saying: "and let us bear your sins." It is a refutation accompanied by a proof. For if their following their way and their turning back from faith in Allah were a sin, it would be a sin before Allah attached to those who turned back. Its transfer from their responsibility to others would require permission and pleasure from Allah. He is the One Who takes them to account for it and requites them, and He, the Glorified, explicitly says: "They will not bear any of their sins," generalizing the negation for every single thing of their sins.

His saying: "They are indeed liars" (إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ) is a deeming of them as liars because their saying "and let us bear your sins" includes an implicit claim that their sins would be transferred to them if they bore them, and that Allah would permit that for them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَيَحْمِلُنَّ أَثْقالَهُمْ وَأَثْقالاً مَعَ أَثْقالِهِمْ وَلَيُسْئَلُنَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ عَمَّا كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ — "They will surely carry their own loads and [other] loads along with their own loads, and they will surely be questioned on the Day of Resurrection about what they used to fabricate" (29:13). This is a completion of the previous speech in refuting them, and it is in the position of a rectification (استدراك). That is, they do not carry their [the believers'] sins themselves, for those remain attached to their doers; however, they will carry loads and burdens of sins like the sins of their doers, without anything being diminished from the doers. They carry them in addition to the loads and burdens of their own souls because they are astray and lead others astray.

Thus, the verse is in the sense of His saying, the Exalted: لِيَحْمِلُوا أَوْزارَهُمْ كامِلَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَمِنْ أَوْزارِ الَّذِينَ يُضِلُّونَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ — "that they may bear their burdens in full on the Day of Resurrection, along with some of the burdens of those whom they lead astray without any knowledge" (16:25).

His saying: "and they will surely be questioned on the Day of Resurrection about what they used to fabricate" (وَلَيُسْئَلُنَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ عَمَّا كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ). Their polytheism is a fabrication against Allah, the Glorified, as is their claim of ability to fulfill what they promised and that Allah would permit that for them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn al-Daris, al-Nahhas, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala’il narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, and also Ibn Mardawayh from ‘Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr, both said: Surat al-‘Ankabut was revealed in Mecca.

I say: It has been narrated in Ruh al-Ma‘ani from al-Bahr from Ibn ‘Abbas that the Surah is Medinan.

In al-Majma‘: It is said the verse—meaning His saying, the Exalted: "Do the people suppose that they will be let off"—was revealed regarding ‘Ammar ibn Yasir, who was being tortured for the sake of Allah. Narrated from Ibn Jurayj.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Sha‘bi regarding His saying: الم أَحَسِبَ النَّاسُ أَنْ يُتْرَكُوا (29:1-2). He said: It was revealed regarding people in Mecca who had acknowledged Islam. The companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) wrote to them from Medina—when the verse of Migration was revealed—that no acknowledgment or Islam would be accepted from you until you migrate. He said: So they set out intending for Medina, but the polytheists followed them and turned them back. So this verse was revealed regarding them. They [the companions] wrote to them that such-and-such a verse was revealed regarding you. They said: We shall go out, and if anyone follows us, we will fight him. So they went out and the polytheists followed them and fought them. Some of them were killed and some were saved. So Allah revealed regarding them: ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما فُتِنُوا ثُمَّ جاهَدُوا وَصَبَرُوا إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنْ بَعْدِها لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — "Then indeed your Lord, to those who migrated after they were persecuted, then strove and were patient—indeed your Lord, after that, is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (16:110).

In the same source: Ibn Jarir narrated from Qatadah: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَقُولُ آمَنَّا بِاللهِ until His saying وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ (29:10-11). He said: These verses were revealed regarding the people whom the polytheists turned back to Mecca, and these ten verses are Medinan.

In the same source: Ibn Jarir narrated from al-Dahhak regarding His saying: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَقُولُ آمَنَّا بِاللهِ. He said: People from the hypocrites in Mecca used to believe, but when they were harmed and affliction from the polytheists befell them, they returned to disbelief and polytheism out of fear of those who harmed them; and they regarded the harm of people in this world as the punishment of Allah.

In the same source: Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Sa‘d ibn Abi Waqqas, who said: My mother said, "I will not eat food nor drink any drink until you disbelieve in Muhammad." She abstained from food and drink until they began to force her mouth open with a stick. So this verse was revealed: وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسانَ بِوالِدَيْهِ حُسْناً (29:8).

In al-Majma‘: al-Kalbi said: His saying: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَقُولُ (29:10) was revealed regarding ‘Ayyash ibn Abi Rabi‘ah al-Makhzumi. That was because he accepted Islam and his family feared [for him]. He migrated to Medina before the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) migrated. His mother, Asma’ bint Mukharramah ibn Abi Jandal al-Tamimi, swore that she would not eat, drink, wash her head, or enter a shelter until he returned to her. When her two sons, Abu Jahl and al-Harith, the sons of Hisham—who were ‘Ayyash’s brothers from his mother—saw her distress, they rode in search of him until they reached Medina. They met him and told him the story. They did not cease until he took covenants from them that they would not turn him away from his religion, and he followed them. His mother had been patient for three days and then ate and drank.

When they left Medina, they seized him and bound him tightly. Each of them lashed him a hundred times until he renounced the religion of Muhammad out of distress from the beating, and he said what was not appropriate. So the verse was revealed. Al-Harith was the harsher of the two toward him. ‘Ayyash swore that if he were able to catch him outside the Sanctuary (الحرم), he would strike his neck.

When they returned to Mecca, they stayed for a while. Then the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers migrated to Medina. ‘Ayyash migrated and his Islam became good. Al-Harith ibn Hisham also accepted Islam and migrated to Medina, and he pledged allegiance to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) upon Islam. ‘Ayyash was not present. One day, ‘Ayyash met him at the back of Quba’, not realizing he had accepted Islam, and he struck his neck. It was said to him, "The man had accepted Islam." ‘Ayyash said, "To Allah we belong and to Him we shall return," and he wept. Then he came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and informed him of that. So there was revealed: وَما كانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ أَنْ يَقْتُلَ مُؤْمِناً إِلَّا خَطأً — "A believer may not kill a believer except by mistake" (4:92).

I say: You see the difference in the reports regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) of the verses. It has already been mentioned that what the context of the verses of the Surah provides is that it is purely Meccan.

In al-Kafi: A number of our companions from Ahmad ibn Muhammad from Mu‘ammar ibn Khallad, who said: I heard Abu al-Hasan (عليه‌السلام) saying: الم أَحَسِبَ النَّاسُ أَنْ يُتْرَكُوا أَنْ يَقُولُوا آمَنَّا ـ وَهُمْ لا يُفْتَنُونَ.

Then he said to me: "What is the tribulation (الفتنة)?" I said: "May I be your ransom, tribulation in religion." He said: "يفتنون كما يفتن الذهب" — "They are tested just as gold is tested." Then he said: "يخلصون كما يخلص الذهب" — "They are purified just as gold is purified."

In al-Majma‘: It is said that the meaning of "they are tested" is that they are tried in their souls and their wealth. This is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام).

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعاً — "or confound you in sects" (6:65). In the Tafsir of al-Kalbi, it is mentioned that when this verse was revealed, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) stood up, performed ablution and perfected it, then stood and prayed, perfecting his prayer. Then he asked Allah, the Glorified, not to send a punishment from above them, or from beneath their feet, or to confound them in sects, or to make some of them taste the violence of others.

Gabriel descended and did not grant him protection from the latter two traits. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "O Gabriel, what will remain of my community if they kill one another?" He stood and returned to supplication. Then there was revealed: الم أَحَسِبَ النَّاسُ أَنْ يُتْرَكُوا (29:1-2). He said: "There must be a tribulation by which the community is tried after its Prophet, so that the truthful may be distinguished from the liar; for revelation has ceased and the sword remains, and the division of the word until the Day of Resurrection."

In Nahj al-Balaghah: A man stood up to him [Imam ‘Ali] and said, "Inform us about the tribulation (الفتنة), and did you ask the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about it?" He (عليه‌السلام) said: "When Allah, the Glorified, revealed His saying: الم أَحَسِبَ النَّاسُ أَنْ يُتْرَكُوا أَنْ يَقُولُوا آمَنَّا وَهُمْ لا يُفْتَنُونَ, I knew that the tribulation would not descend upon us while the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was among us. So I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, what is this tribulation that Allah has informed you of?' He said: 'O ‘Ali, indeed my community will be tried after me.'"

In al-Tawhid: From ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) in a long tradition, where a man asked him about verses from the Qur’an and His saying: مَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ اللهِ فَإِنَّ أَجَلَ اللهِ لَآتٍ. He said: "It means by His saying: 'Whoever believes that he will be resurrected,' for the promise of Allah of reward and punishment is coming. The encounter (اللقاء) here is not by vision; the encounter is the Resurrection (البعث). So understand all that is in the Book of Allah regarding His encounter, for He means by that the Resurrection."

I say: His (عليه‌السلام) intention is to negate sensory vision and to provide an interpretation by the necessary implication of the meaning.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi regarding His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ اللهِ (29:5). He said: "Whoever loves the encounter with Allah, the appointed time comes to him." وَمَنْ جاهَدَ (29:6) "his soul against pleasures, desires, and sins," فإِنَّما يُجاهِدُ لِنَفْسِهِ إِنَّ اللهَ لَغَنِيٌّ عَنِ الْعالَمِينَ. وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسانَ بِوالِدَيْهِ حُسْناً (29:8). He said: "They are the two who gave birth to him."

In the same source regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ـ اتَّبِعُوا سَبِيلَنا وَلْنَحْمِلْ خَطاياكُمْ (29:12). He said: "The disbelievers used to say to the believers: 'Be with us, for that which you fear is nothing; if it be true, we will bear your sins for you.' So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, punishes them twice: once for their own sins and once for the sins of others."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Abi Shaybah in al-Musannaf and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn al-Hanafiyyah, who said: Abu Jahl and the chiefs of Quraysh used to meet people when they came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to accept Islam, saying: "He forbids wine, forbids adultery, and forbids what the Arabs used to do. So return, and we will bear your burdens." So this verse was revealed: وَلَيَحْمِلُنَّ أَثْقالَهُمْ وَأَثْقالاً مَعَ أَثْقالِهِمْ (29:13).

In the same source: Ahmad narrated from Hudhayfah, who said: A man asked [for charity] during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the people held back. Then a man gave to him, and the people [then] gave. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "من سن خيرا فاستن به ـ كان له أجره ومن أجور من تبعه ـ غير منتقص من أجورهم شيئا ، ومن سن شرا فاستن به كان عليه وزره ومن أوزار من تبعه ـ غير منتقص من أوزارهم شيئا" — "Whoever establishes a good practice (سنة) which is then followed, he shall have its reward and a portion of the rewards of those who follow him, without anything being diminished from their rewards. And whoever establishes an evil practice which is then followed, he shall bear its burden and a portion of the burdens of those who follow him, without anything being diminished from their burdens."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning, and in some of them is the interpretation of His saying: "They will surely carry their own loads and [other] loads along with their own loads" by that.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فَلَبِثَ فِيهِمْ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ إِلَّا خَمْسِينَ عَامًا فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱلطُّوفَانُ وَهُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (١٤)
Certainly We sent Noah to his people, and he remained with them for a thousand-less-fifty years. Then the flood overtook them while they were wrongdoers. (14)
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَأَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلسَّفِينَةِ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهَآ ءَايَةً لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٥)
Then We delivered him and those who were in the Ark, and made it a sign for all the nations. (15)
وَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱتَّقُوهُ ۖ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (١٦)
And Abraham, when he said to his people, ‘Worship Allah and be wary of Him. That is better for you, should you know. (16)
إِنَّمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْثَـٰنًا وَتَخْلُقُونَ إِفْكًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ لَكُمْ رِزْقًا فَٱبْتَغُوا۟ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرِّزْقَ وَٱعْبُدُوهُ وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لَهُۥٓ ۖ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (١٧)
What you worship instead of Allah are mere idols, and you invent a lie. Indeed those whom you worship besides Allah have no control over your provision. So seek all [your] provision from Allah, and worship Him and thank Him, and to Him you shall be brought back.’ (17)
وَإِن تُكَذِّبُوا۟ فَقَدْ كَذَّبَ أُمَمٌ مِّن قَبْلِكُمْ ۖ وَمَا عَلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٨)
If you impugn [the Apostle’s teaching], then [other] nations have impugned [likewise] before you, and the Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms. (18)
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ كَيْفَ يُبْدِئُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ (١٩)
Have they not regarded how Allah originates the creation? Then He will bring it back. That is indeed easy for Allah. (19)
قُلْ سِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ بَدَأَ ٱلْخَلْقَ ۚ ثُمَّ ٱللَّهُ يُنشِئُ ٱلنَّشْأَةَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢٠)
Say, ‘Travel over the land and observe how He has originated the creation.’ Then Allah will bring about the genesis of the Hereafter. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (20)
يُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَرْحَمُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ تُقْلَبُونَ (٢١)
He will punish whomever He wishes and have mercy on whomever He wishes, and to Him you will be returned. (21)
وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۖ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (٢٢)
You cannot frustrate Him on the earth or in the sky, nor do you have besides Allah any guardian or helper. (22)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِقَآئِهِۦٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَئِسُوا۟ مِن رَّحْمَتِى وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٢٣)
Those who deny the signs of Allah and the encounter with Him—they have despaired of My mercy, and for such there is a painful punishment. (23)
فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ ٱقْتُلُوهُ أَوْ حَرِّقُوهُ فَأَنجَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٢٤)
But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Kill him, or burn him.’ Then Allah delivered him from the fire. There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith. (24)
وَقَالَ إِنَّمَا ٱتَّخَذْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْثَـٰنًا مَّوَدَّةَ بَيْنِكُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ ثُمَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يَكْفُرُ بَعْضُكُم بِبَعْضٍ وَيَلْعَنُ بَعْضُكُم بَعْضًا وَمَأْوَىٰكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٢٥)
He said, ‘You have taken idols [for worship] besides Allah for the sake of [mutual] affection amongst yourselves in the life of the world. Then on the Day of Resurrection you will disown one another and curse one another, and the Fire will be your abode, and you will not have any helpers.’ (25)
۞ فَـَٔامَنَ لَهُۥ لُوطٌ ۘ وَقَالَ إِنِّى مُهَاجِرٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّىٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢٦)
Thereupon Lot believed in him, and he said, ‘Indeed I am migrating toward my Lord. Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-wise.’ (26)
وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَجَعَلْنَا فِى ذُرِّيَّتِهِ ٱلنُّبُوَّةَ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ أَجْرَهُۥ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٢٧)
And We gave him Isaac and Jacob, and We ordained among his descendants prophethood and the Book, and We gave him his reward in this world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous. (27)
وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُم بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٨)
And Lot, when he said to his people, ‘You indeed commit an indecency none in the world has ever committed before you! (28)
أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ ٱلرِّجَالَ وَتَقْطَعُونَ ٱلسَّبِيلَ وَتَأْتُونَ فِى نَادِيكُمُ ٱلْمُنكَرَ ۖ فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ ٱئْتِنَا بِعَذَابِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٢٩)
What! Do you come to men, and cut off the way, and commit outrages in your gatherings?’ But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Bring down us Allah’s punishment, if you are truthful.’ (29)
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱنصُرْنِى عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٣٠)
He said, ‘My Lord! Help me against this corruptive lot.’ (30)
وَلَمَّا جَآءَتْ رُسُلُنَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ بِٱلْبُشْرَىٰ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مُهْلِكُوٓا۟ أَهْلِ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ۖ إِنَّ أَهْلَهَا كَانُوا۟ ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٣١)
And when Our messengers came to Abraham with the good news, they said, ‘We are indeed going to destroy the people of this town. Its people are indeed wrongdoers.’ (31)
قَالَ إِنَّ فِيهَا لُوطًا ۚ قَالُوا۟ نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن فِيهَا ۖ لَنُنَجِّيَنَّهُۥ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَهُۥ كَانَتْ مِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (٣٢)
He said, ‘Lot is in it.’ They said, ‘We know better those who are in it. We will surely deliver him and his family, except his wife: she shall be one of those who remain behind.’ (32)
وَلَمَّآ أَن جَآءَتْ رُسُلُنَا لُوطًا سِىٓءَ بِهِمْ وَضَاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعًا وَقَالُوا۟ لَا تَخَفْ وَلَا تَحْزَنْ ۖ إِنَّا مُنَجُّوكَ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَكَ كَانَتْ مِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (٣٣)
And when Our messengers came to Lot, he was distressed on their account and in a predicament for their sake. But they said, ‘Do not be afraid, nor grieve! We shall deliver you and your family, except your wife: she will be one of those who remain behind. (33)
إِنَّا مُنزِلُونَ عَلَىٰٓ أَهْلِ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ رِجْزًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ (٣٤)
We are indeed going to bring down upon the people of this town a punishment from the sky because of the transgressions they used to commit.’ (34)
وَلَقَد تَّرَكْنَا مِنْهَآ ءَايَةًۢ بَيِّنَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (٣٥)
Certainly We have left of it a manifest sign for people who exercise their reason. (35)
وَإِلَىٰ مَدْيَنَ أَخَاهُمْ شُعَيْبًا فَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱرْجُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ (٣٦)
And to Midian We sent Shu‘ayb, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah, and expect [to encounter] the Last Day, and do not act wickedly on the earth causing corruption.’ (36)
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دَارِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (٣٧)
But they impugned him, whereupon the earthquake seized them, and they lay lifeless prostrate in their homes. (37)
وَعَادًا وَثَمُودَا۟ وَقَد تَّبَيَّنَ لَكُم مِّن مَّسَـٰكِنِهِمْ ۖ وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَكَانُوا۟ مُسْتَبْصِرِينَ (٣٨)
And ‘Ad and Thamud, [whose fate] is evident to you from their habitations. Satan made their deeds seem decorous to them, thus he barred them from the way [of Allah], though they used to be perceptive. (38)
وَقَـٰرُونَ وَفِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ ۖ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَهُم مُّوسَىٰ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ سَـٰبِقِينَ (٣٩)
And Korah, Pharaoh, and Haman. Certainly, Moses brought them manifest proofs, but they acted arrogantly in the land; though they could not outmaneuver [Allah]. (39)
فَكُلًّا أَخَذْنَا بِذَنۢبِهِۦ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِ حَاصِبًا وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ أَخَذَتْهُ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ خَسَفْنَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ أَغْرَقْنَا ۚ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٤٠)
So We seized each [of them] for his sin: among them were those upon whom We unleashed a rain of stones, and among them were those who were seized by the Cry, and among them were those whom We caused the earth to swallow, and among them were those whom We drowned. It was not Allah who wronged them, but it was they who used to wrong themselves. (40)

(Commentary)

When the Glorified mentioned at the beginning of the chapter that trial (الفتنة) — the divine system of testing and tribulation — is a divine sunnah (سنة إلهية) from which there is no deviation, and that it had occurred in previous nations, He followed that by referring to the stories of seven of the past prophets and their nations. They are: Nuh, Ibrahim, Lut, Shu‘ayb, Hud, Salih, and Musa (peace be upon him). Allah tried and tested them; thus, those among them who were saved were saved, and those who perished perished. The Glorified mentioned both salvation and destruction in the first three stories, while in the last four, He mentioned only destruction.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَى قَوْمِهِ فَلَبِثَ فِيهِمْ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ إِلَّا خَمْسِينَ عَامًا فَأَخَذَهُمُ الطُّوفَانُ وَهُمْ ظَالِمُونَ — "Certainly We sent Noah to his people, and he remained among them for a thousand-years-less-fifty. Then the flood overtook them while they were wrongdoers." (29:14). In al-Majma‘: The flood (الطوفان) is abundant, overwhelming water, so called because it "circles" (يطوف) the regions of the earth due to its vastness. It is also said: It is anything that surrounds a thing with abundance and intensity, whether it be a torrent, wind, or darkness, though it is most commonly used for a flood of water.

The expression "a thousand years less fifty" (ألف سنة إلا خمسين عاماً) rather than saying "nine hundred and fifty years" is for emphasis of the great length (التكثير). The verse is apparent in that the "thousand less fifty" was the duration of Nuh’s (peace be upon him) mission (الدعوة) from his appointment until the flood took them. This differs from what is in the current Torah, which claims it was the duration of his entire life (عمره). Reference to this has already been made in his stories in the commentary on Surah Hud, and the rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَنْجَيْنَاهُ وَأَصْحَابَ السَّفِينَةِ وَجَعَلْنَاهَا آيَةً لِلْعَالَمِينَ — "Then We delivered him and the occupants of the Ark, and made it a sign for all the worlds." (29:15). That is, We saved Nuh and the companions of the Ark who rode with him, who were his family and a small number of believers, and they were not among the wrongdoers.

His saying: وَجَعَلْنَاهَا آيَةً لِلْعَالَمِينَ — "and made it a sign for all the worlds." It is apparent that the pronoun refers to the event (الواقعة) or the salvation (النجاة). As for it referring back to the Ark (السفينة), it is somewhat far-fetched. All the worlds (العالمين) refers to the many diverse groups of subsequent generations.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِبْرَاهِيمَ إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَاتَّقُوهُ ذَلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ — "And Abraham, when he said to his people, ‘Worship Allah and be wary of Him. That is better for you, should you know." (29:16). This is conjoined to His saying: نُوحًا (Nuh), meaning: And We sent Ibrahim to his people.

His saying to his people: اعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَاتَّقُوهُ — "Worship Allah and be wary of Him" is a call to monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and a warning, as evidenced by the subsequent verses; thus, the sentence conveys the sense of exclusivity (الحصر).

Furthermore, the idolaters (الوثنية) do not worship Allah, the Glorified; they only worship others based on their claim that He, the Exalted, cannot be worshipped except through the effective causes in the world that are near to Him, such as the angels and the jinn. Had He been worshipped, He would be worshipped alone without any partner. Thus, calling them to the worship of Allah by saying: اعْبُدُوا اللهَ (Worship Allah) implies calling to Him alone, even if it is not restricted by a particle of exclusivity.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَوْثَانًا وَتَخْلُقُونَ إِفْكًا — "What you worship instead of Allah are only idols, and you invent a lie..." (29:17) to the end of the verse. Idols (الأوثان) is the plural of wathan (وثن), which is a statue (الصنم). Falsehood (الإفك) is a matter turned away from its proper face, whether in speech or action.

His saying: إِنَّمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَوْثَانًا — "What you worship instead of Allah are only idols" is an exposition of the futility of idol worship, through which it becomes clear that the worship of Allah is the true worship; in short, the restriction of true worship to Him, the Exalted. The word "idols" (أوثاناً) is indefinite (منكر) to indicate the weakness of their status and that their divinity is a mere claim with no reality behind it. That is: You worship nothing besides Allah except idols whose state is such-and-such.

Therefore, He followed the sentence with His saying: وَتَخْلُقُونَ إِفْكًا — "and you invent a lie," meaning you fabricate a falsehood by naming them gods and then worshipping them. Thus, there is a God whose worship is obligatory, but He is Allah, the One, and not the idols.

His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ لَكُمْ رِزْقًا — "Indeed those whom you worship besides Allah have no control over your provision." (29:17). This is a justification (تعليل) for what was mentioned regarding their fabrication of lies by naming idols as gods and worshipping them. The gist is that those whom you worship besides Allah—the idols, insofar as they are images of the "near ones" among the angels and jinn—you only worship them to gain benefit, which is that they might be pleased with you and thus provide for you and bestow sustenance upon you. However, they do not possess any provision for you.

For Allah is the One who possesses your provision (الرزق), which is the cause that sustains your existence, because He is the One who created you and created your provision, making it the sustainer of your survival. Ownership (الملك) follows creation (الخلق) and origination (الإيجاد).

Therefore, He followed it with His saying: فَابْتَغُوا عِنْدَ اللهِ الرِّزْقَ وَاعْبُدُوهُ وَاشْكُرُوا لَهُ — "So seek [your] provision from Allah, and worship Him and thank Him." (29:17). That is: Seek provision from Allah because He is the One who possesses it; so do not worship them, but worship Allah and be grateful to Him for what He has provided you and the various blessings He has bestowed upon you. It is obligatory to thank the Benefactor (المنعم) for what He has bestowed.

His saying: إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ — "To Him you shall be brought back" (29:17) is in the position of justification for His saying: وَاعْبُدُوهُ وَاشْكُرُوا لَهُ (and worship Him and thank Him). For this reason, it was brought as a separate clause without a conjunction. In this justification, He turns them away from worshipping the Deity for the sake of seeking provision toward worshipping Him for the sake of the Return (الرجوع) and the Reckoning (الحساب). For were it not for the Resurrection (المعاد), there would be no realized cause for worshipping the Deity, since provision and its like have specific cosmic causes (أسباب كونية) other than acts of worship and devotions, and it does not increase or decrease by faith or disbelief. However, the felicity of the Day of Reckoning differs based on faith, disbelief, worship, and gratitude. Therefore, let the return to Allah be the motive for worship and gratitude, rather than the seeking of provision.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ تُكَذِّبُوا فَقَدْ كَذَّبَ أُمَمٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَمَا عَلَى الرَّسُولِ إِلَّا الْبَلَاغُ الْمُبِينُ — "And if you impugn [me], then nations before you have impugned [their apostles], and the Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms." (29:18). It is apparent that this is part of the speech of Ibrahim (peace be upon him). Some have mentioned that it is a discourse from Him, the Exalted, to the polytheists of the Quraysh, but this is not without some far-fetchedness.

The meaning of the condition and its result at the beginning of the verse is that impugnment (التكذيب) is what is expected from you, for it is like a prevailing sunnah among the polytheistic nations; nations before you have impugned, and you are of them and among the last of them. And there is nothing upon me, in my capacity as a messenger, except the clear communication (البلاغ المبين).

It is possible that the meaning is that your state in your impugnment is like the state of the nations before you; their impugnment did not benefit them at all when the punishment of Allah befell them, and they could not escape in the earth or in the heaven, nor did they have any guardian or helper besides Allah. So shall it be with you. His saying: وَمَا عَلَى الرَّسُولِ (and the Apostle’s duty is only...) is suitable for both interpretations.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا كَيْفَ يُبْدِئُ اللهُ الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ إِنَّ ذَلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ — "Have they not seen how Allah originates the creation and then repeats it? That is indeed easy for Allah." (29:19). This verse, up to the completion of five verses, is from the speech of Him, the Exalted, occurring within the story to establish the proof for the Resurrection (المعاد) — the return of the soul to the body for judgment — and to remove their deeming it improbable. It is connected to what preceded insofar as the primary reason for their impugning the messengers was their denial of the Resurrection, as indicated by Ibrahim’s saying: إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ وَإِنْ تُكَذِّبُوا فَقَدْ كَذَّبَ أُمَمٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ (To Him you shall be brought back. And if you impugn...).

So His saying: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا (Have they not seen...) etc., the pronoun refers to the impugners among all nations, past and future. The "seeing" (الرؤية) intended here is intellectual perception (النظر العلمي) rather than visual sight. His saying: كَيْفَ يُبْدِئُ اللهُ الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ (how Allah originates the creation and then repeats it) is in the position of the object for His saying: يَرَوْا (they see), with "repeats it" (يعيده) conjoined to the position of "originates" (يبدئ), contrary to those who see it conjoined to "Have they not seen." The interrogative is for rebuke (التوبيخ).

The meaning is: Have they not known the modality of origination and then repetition? That is, they are of the same genus, which is the bringing into existence of that which was not. His saying: إِنَّ ذَلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ — "That is indeed easy for Allah." The reference here is to the repetition after the origination. In this is the removal of the sense of improbability, because it is an origination after an origination. Since absolute power (القدرة المطلقة) relates to bringing into existence (الإيجاد), it is permissible for it to relate to origination after origination. In reality, it is a transfer of creation from one abode to another, and the settling of the travelers toward Him in the Abode of Permanence (دار القرار).

The saying of some that the meaning of origination and then repetition is the origination of creation and then the creation of their likes after their annihilation is not sound, because it does not suit the argumentation for the Resurrection, which is the return of the very thing that perished, not its like.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ بَدَأَ الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ اللهُ يُنْشِئُ النَّشْأَةَ الْآخِرَةَ إِنَّ اللهَ عَلَى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ — "Say, ‘Travel over the land and observe how He has originated the creation.’ Then Allah will bring about the next genesis. Indeed Allah has power over all things." (29:20). This verse, up to the completion of three verses, is a command to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to address them with that which completes the proof against them. He guides them to travel through the earth to observe how He began the creation and originated them despite their differing natures, varying colors, and forms, without a prior model or restriction or limit in their number or preparation. In this is a proof of the lack of limitation in divine power; thus, He brings about the next genesis (النشأة الآخرة) just as He originated the first genesis. The verse is similar in meaning to His saying: وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ النَّشْأَةَ الْأُولَى فَلَوْلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ — "Certainly you have known the first genesis, then why do you not take admonition?" (56:62).

His saying, the Exalted: يُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ وَيَرْحَمُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُقْلَبُونَ — "He punishes whomever He wishes and has mercy on whomever He wishes, and to Him you will be returned." (29:21). This is part of the commanded speech. It is apparent that it is an exposition of His saying: يُنْشِئُ النَّشْأَةَ الْآخِرَةَ (He will bring about the next genesis). To "turn" (قلب) a thing is to transform it from its face or state, such as making its bottom its top or making its interior its exterior. This latter meaning suits His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرَائِرُ — "on the day when the secrets are examined." (86:9).

They have interpreted "turning" (القلب) as returning (الرد). It is said in al-Majma‘: al-Qalb is the return and the sending back; so its meaning is that you are returned to the state of life in the Hereafter where no one possesses benefit or harm except Allah. This is a subtle meaning by which the sense of returning to Allah and being sent back to Him is explained: it is their standing in a position where causes (الأسباب) are severed from them and none rules therein except Allah, the Glorified. Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to His saying: وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلَاهُمُ الْحَقِّ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ مَا كَانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ — "and they will be returned to Allah, their true Master, and what they used to fabricate will forsake them." (10:30).

The gist of the meaning is: The next genesis is a genesis in which Allah punishes whom He wills—who are the criminals—and has mercy on whom He wills—who are others—and to Him you are returned, so none other than Him shall rule over you.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِي السَّمَاءِ وَمَا لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ — "You cannot frustrate [Him] in the earth or in the sky, nor do you have any guardian or helper besides Allah." (29:22). This is part of the commanded speech and a description of their state on the Day of Resurrection, just as the previous verse was a description of His state, the Exalted, on that day.

So His saying: وَمَا أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِي السَّمَاءِ — "You cannot frustrate [Him] in the earth or in the sky" means that you are not able to frustrate Him, the Exalted, on that day by escaping Him or exiting from His judgment and authority through flight, or by leaving His kingdom and penetrating the regions of the earth and the sky. Thus, the verse follows the course of His saying: يَا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ إِنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ أَنْ تَنْفُذُوا مِنْ أَقْطَارِ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ فَانْفُذُوا — "O company of jinn and humans! If you can pass through the confines of the heavens and the earth, then do pass!" (55:33).

It is said: The speech is in the sense of "those in the sky," so "those" was omitted because the context indicates it, and the estimation is: "You cannot frustrate [Him] in the earth, nor can those in the sky frustrate [Him] in the sky."

This is far-fetched, and the context's indication of it is not conceded. Even if it were based on this, it would suffice that the discourse is for the generality of creation by giving preponderance (تغليب) to the human side addressed over others like the jinn and angels. The meaning would be: "You, O assembly of creation, cannot frustrate [Him] in the earth or in the sky."

His saying: وَمَا لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ — "nor do you have any guardian or helper besides Allah." That is: You have no guardian (ولي) today besides Allah to take charge of your affairs and suffice you against Allah, nor any helper (نصير) to assist you, strengthen your side, and complete your deficient power so as to grant you victory over Him, the Glorified.

The verse—as you see—negates their prevailing over Allah or frustrating Him by exiting and refusing His judgment in all its forms. They do not independently achieve this, which is His saying: وَمَا أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ (You cannot frustrate...), nor do others independently achieve it, which is His saying: وَمَا لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ (nor do you have... any guardian), nor does the collective of them and others frustrate Him, the Exalted, which is His saying: وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (nor any helper).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآيَاتِ اللهِ وَلِقَائِهِ أُولَئِكَ يَئِسُوا مِنْ رَحْمَتِي وَأُولَئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ — "As for those who deny the signs of Allah and the encounter with Him, they have despaired of My mercy, and for such there is a painful punishment." (29:23). This is a discourse directed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), outside of the previous commanded speech "Say, 'Travel over the land...'" etc. What is sought therein is to inform him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) of the explicit truth regarding who shall be wretched and perish on the Day of Resurrection, for He had left that ambiguous in His first saying: يُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ وَيَرْحَمُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ (He punishes whomever He wishes and has mercy on whomever He wishes).

Evidence for this is the address in "those" (أُولَئِكَ) twice; had it been from the speech of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), it would have been said: "those of you" (أولئكم).

This is also supported by His saying: مِنْ رَحْمَتِي (of My mercy). For the transition from something like our saying: "Those have despaired of the mercy of Allah" or "of His mercy" in the third-person context as required by the situation, to His saying: أُولَئِكَ يَئِسُوا مِنْ رَحْمَتِي (they have despaired of My mercy) conveys confirmation and acknowledgment in addition to the original report. Thus, it provides an explicit designation of the people of punishment. This is further supported by the repetition of the demonstrative pronoun and the emphasis in the context.

In specifying the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with this report, there was a strengthening of his noble soul and a sidelining of them from being fit to hear the like of it, as they do not believe.

The meaning of "the signs of Allah" (آيات الله)—based on the absolute nature of the term—is all the proofs indicating Unity (الوحدانية), Prophethood (النبوة), and the Resurrection (المعاد), including cosmic signs, prophetic miracles, and among them the Quran. Thus, disbelief in the signs of Allah includes, by its generality, disbelief in the Resurrection. Mentioning disbelief in the "encounter" (اللقاء), which is the Resurrection, after disbelief in the signs is the mention of the specific after the general. The reason for this is to indicate the importance of belief in the Resurrection, since with the denial of the Resurrection, the matter of the true religion becomes futile from its foundation, as is apparent.

The meaning of "mercy" (الرحمة) is that which contrasts with punishment and is inseparable from Paradise (الجنة). The application of "mercy" to Paradise has been repeated in His speech by way of necessity, such as His saying: فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ — "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their Lord will admit them into His mercy." (45:30) and His saying: يُدْخِلُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ وَالظَّالِمِينَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا — "He admits whomever He wishes into His mercy, and for the wrongdoers He has prepared a painful punishment." (76:31).

The meaning of attributing "despair" (اليأس) to them is either their actual engagement in it, for by their denial of the afterlife, they are despairing of eternal felicity and the everlasting Paradise; or it is a metaphor (كناية) for His final decree that no disbeliever shall enter Paradise.

The meaning is: Those who denied the signs of Allah indicating the true religion, and especially the Resurrection, those have despaired of mercy and Paradise, and for those is a painful punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ قَالُوا اقْتُلُوهُ أَوْ حَرِّقُوهُ فَأَنْجَاهُ اللهُ مِنَ النَّارِ — "But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Kill him, or burn him!’ Then Allah delivered him from the fire..." (29:24). This is a derivation from His saying at the beginning of the story: وَإِبْرَاهِيمَ إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَاتَّقُوهُ (And Abraham, when he said to his people, ‘Worship Allah and be wary of Him’).

The appearance of His saying: قَالُوا اقْتُلُوهُ أَوْ حَرِّقُوهُ (they said, ‘Kill him, or burn him!’) is that each side of the alternative was the saying of a group among them. What was meant by killing was killing with the sword and its like; this was their saying when they first conspired to punish him, even if they later agreed upon burning him, as He said: قَالُوا حَرِّقُوهُ وَانْصُرُوا آلِهَتَكُمْ — "They said, ‘Burn him, and help your gods...’" (21:68). It is also possible that the alternative came from all of them due to their initial hesitation regarding his matter, followed by their agreement on burning him.

His saying: فأَنْجَاهُ اللهُ مِنَ النَّارِ — "Then Allah delivered him from the fire" contains an omission and brevity (إيجاز). Its estimation is: Then they agreed to burn him, so they kindled a fire and threw him into it, and Allah saved him from it. The story has been detailed in various places in His speech.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالَ إِنَّمَا اتَّخَذْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَوْثَانًا مَوَدَّةَ بَيْنِكُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا — "He said, ‘You have taken idols [for worship] besides Allah for the sake of affection between you in the life of the world..." (29:25) to the end of the verse.
