Since they had no intellectual proof for taking idols, nothing remained for them to follow as a tradition (سنة) except the following of the tradition of those they aggrandize and respect, such as fathers for sons, honored leaders for their followers, friends for their friends, and ultimately, the nation for its individuals. This connecting cause is the mainstay of what preserves national traditions, keeping them practiced and established.

Following the tradition of idolatry is, in reality, one of the effects of social death; the common folk see it from one another, and national affection (المودة القومية) prompts them to imitate and follow the tradition like them. Then, this very following preserves national affection and maintains unity and agreement.

This is the state of the commoners among them. As for the elite, they might rely on what they consider a proof, though it is no proof, such as their saying that Allah, the Glorified, is too majestic to be encompassed by sense, imagination, or intellect, so our devotional orientation cannot relate to Him. Thus, it is obligatory that we draw near to some of those who have favor with Him, like the angels and jinn, so they may bring us closer to Him and intercede for us with Him.

So His saying: إِنَّمَا اتَّخَذْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَوْثَانًا مَوَدَّةَ بَيْنِكُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا — "You have taken idols [for worship] besides Allah for the sake of affection between you in the life of the world" is a discourse from him (peace be upon him) to the generality of his people regarding their taking of idols for national affection, to rectify thereby the affairs of their social worldly life. They had answered him with this when he asked them about their state: إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِ مَا هَذِهِ التَّمَاثِيلُ الَّتِي أَنْتُمْ لَهَا عَاكِفُونَ قَالُوا وَجَدْنَا آبَاءَنَا لَهَا عَابِدِينَ — "When he said to his father and his people, ‘What are these images to which you keep clinging?’ They said, ‘We found our fathers worshipping them.’" (21:52-53); قَالَ هَلْ يَسْمَعُونَكُمْ إِذْ تَدْعُونَ أَوْ يَنْفَعُونَكُمْ أَوْ يَضُرُّونَ قَالُوا بَلْ وَجَدْنَا آبَاءَنَا كَذَلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ — "He said, ‘Do they hear you when you call [them]? Or do they benefit you, or harm?’ They said, ‘No, but we found our fathers doing likewise.’" (26:72-74).

From here it becomes clear that His saying: مَوَدَّةَ بَيْنِكُمْ (affection between you) is suitable to be in the accusative case by the removal of the preposition (منصوباً بنزع الخافض) with the estimation of the "lam" of justification (لام التعليل); affection, on this view, is a cause leading to the taking of idols. It is also suitable to be an object of purpose (مفعولاً له), where affection is the intended goal of taking idols. However, the end of the verse only aligns with the second view, as will become clear.

Then he (peace be upon him) followed His saying: إِنَّمَا اتَّخَذْتُمْ (You have only taken...) etc., with His saying: ثُمَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَكْفُرُ بَعْضُكُمْ بِبَعْضٍ وَيَلْعَنُ بَعْضُكُمْ بَعْضًا — "Then on the Day of Resurrection you will disown one another and curse one another" (29:25). He explains to them the consequence of taking idols for affection, which is the inner reality of this intended affection that will appear on the day when secrets are examined. For they sought this meager enjoyment through polytheism (الشرك), which is the greatest injustice and the largest of the destructive major sins; they gathered upon it and agreed, but the reality of their deed will appear to them, and its evil consequence will catch up with them. Thus, they will disassociate from one another and deny one another.

What is meant by "some of you disbelieving in others" (يكفر بعضكم ببعض) is their gods disowning them and disassociating from them, as He, the Exalted, said: سَيَكْفُرُونَ بِعِبَادَتِهِمْ وَيَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضِدًّا — "They will soon deny their worship and they will be their adversaries." (19:82) and He said: وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَكْفُرُونَ بِشِرْكِكُمْ — "and on the Day of Resurrection they will deny your polytheism." (35:14). In the same sense is the disassociation of the followed from their followers, as He, the Exalted, said: إِذْ تَبَرَّأَ الَّذِينَ اتُّبِعُوا مِنَ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوا وَرَأَوُا الْعَذَابَ وَتَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ الْأَسْبَابُ — "When those who were followed will disown those who followed [them], and they will sight the punishment and all their means will be cut off." (2:166). What is meant by "some of you cursing others" is each group cursing its companion; He, the Exalted, said: كُلَّمَا دَخَلَتْ أُمَّةٌ لَعَنَتْ أُخْتَهَا — "Every time a nation enters [hell], it will curse its sister [nation]." (7:38).

Then He followed that with His saying: وَمَأْوَاكُمُ النَّارُ وَمَا لَكُمْ مِنْ نَاصِرِينَ — "and your abode will be the Fire, and you will have no helpers." (29:25). This is an indication of the arrival of the evil consequence and the occurrence of the recompense, which is the Fire wherein is eternal destruction, and there is no helper to assist them or repel the punishment from them. They only sought affection to help, cooperate, and support one another in life, but it turned into enmity and opposition on the Day of Resurrection, inheriting disassociation and abandonment.

His saying, the Exalted: فَآمَنَ لَهُ لُوطٌ وَقَالَ إِنِّي مُهَاجِرٌ إِلَى رَبِّي إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ — "Thereupon Lot believed in him, and he said, ‘I am indeed migrating toward my Lord. Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-wise." (29:26). That is, Lut believed in him. Faith (الإيمان) is made transitive with the "lam" just as it is with the "ba," and the meaning is the same.

His saying: وَقَالَ إِنِّي مُهَاجِرٌ إِلَى رَبِّي — "and he said, ‘I am indeed migrating toward my Lord." It is said the pronoun refers to Lut, and it is also said it refers to Ibrahim. The latter is supported by His saying, the Exalted, narrating from Ibrahim: وَقَالَ إِنِّي ذَاهِبٌ إِلَى رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ — "He said, ‘I am going toward my Lord; He will guide me.’" (37:99).

It is as if the meaning of migrating to Allah is leaving his homeland and departing from among his polytheistic people to a land where the polytheists would not oppose him or prevent him from worshipping his Lord. Thus, counting the migration as being "toward Allah" is a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي).

His saying: إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ — "Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-wise." That is, the Mighty (العزيز) who does not let those He helps be humiliated, the Wise (الحكيم) who does not let those He protects be lost.

His saying, the Exalted: وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَجَعَلْنَا فِي ذُرِّيَّتِهِ النُّبُوَّةَ وَالْكِتَابَ — "And We gave him Isaac and Jacob, and We ordained prophethood and the Book among his descendants..." (29:27). Its meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآتَيْنَاهُ أَجْرَهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "and We gave him his reward in this world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous." (29:27). Reward (الأجر) is the recompense that corresponds to an action and returns to its doer. The difference between it and "wage" (الأجرة) is that wage is specific to worldly recompense, while reward (الأجر) encompasses both this world and the Hereafter. The difference between it and "recompense" (الجزاء) is that reward is only used for what is good and beneficial, while recompense encompasses good and evil, beneficial and harmful.

The most common usage in His speech is the use of the word "reward" (الأجر) for the recompense of devotional action that Allah, the Glorified, has prepared for His believing servants in the Hereafter, such as stations of proximity (مقامات القرب), degrees of guardianship (درجات الولاية), and among them Paradise. Yes, it has occurred in His saying, the Exalted, narrating from Yusuf (peace be upon him): إِنَّهُ مَنْ يَتَّقِ وَيَصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ — "Indeed if one is God-wary and patient, Allah does not waste the reward of the virtuous." (12:90) and His saying: وَكَذَلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَتَبَوَّأُ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ يَشَاءُ نُصِيبُ بِرَحْمَتِنَا مَنْ نَشَاءُ وَلَا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ — "Thus We established Joseph in the land, to settle in it wherever he wished. We dispense Our mercy on whomever We wish, and We do not waste the reward of the virtuous." (12:56), applying "reward" to good worldly recompense.

So His saying: وَآتَيْنَاهُ أَجْرَهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا — "and We gave him his reward in this world" could mean the giving of a good worldly reward. In this case, it is more appropriate that "in this world" (في الدنيا) be connected to "reward" rather than to "giving." This meaning is perhaps supported by His saying, the Exalted, regarding him (peace be upon him) in another place: وَآتَيْنَاهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "We gave him good in this world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous." (16:122). For it is apparent that what is meant by "good" (حسنة) is a good life or a good livelihood, and its "giving" is the actual bestowing of it, not just its decree and writing.

It is also possible that what is meant is the advancement of what is prepared for the generality of believers in the Hereafter—namely, the stations of proximity—in his favor (peace be upon him), and giving him that in this world. An enumeration of what the Quran mentions of his stations (peace be upon him) has already preceded in his stories in the commentary on Surah al-An‘am.

His saying: وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous." Discussion on this has preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدِ اصْطَفَيْنَاهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "Certainly We chose him in the present world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous." (2:130) in the first volume of the book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُمْ بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنَ الْعَالَمِينَ — "And Lot, when he said to his people, ‘You indeed commit an indecency none in the world has ever committed before you." (29:28). That is: And We sent Lut, or: And remember Lut when he said to his people. His saying: إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ — "You indeed commit an indecency" is a report given by way of amazement and denunciation. What is meant by "indecency" (الفاحشة) is the approaching of males.

His saying: مَا سَبَقَكُمْ بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنَ الْعَالَمِينَ — "none in the world has ever committed before you" is an initial sentence (استئناف) that clarifies the meaning of the indecency and emphasizes it. It is as if the meaning is that this act had not spread in any nation before them to this extent; or the sentence is a circumstantial clause (حال) for the subject of "you commit" (لتأتون).

His saying, the Exalted: أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجَالَ وَتَقْطَعُونَ السَّبِيلَ وَتَأْتُونَ فِي نَادِيكُمُ الْمُنْكَرَ — "Do you indeed come to men, and cut off the way, and commit abominations in your gatherings?..." (29:29) to the end of the verse. This is an interrogation regarding a matter that a listener should not believe and a person of intellect should not accept; hence it was emphasized with the "nun" and the "lam." This context testifies that what is meant by "coming to men" is sodomy, and by "cutting off the way" is the neglect and cancellation of the path of procreation, which is coming to women. Thus, "cutting off the way" is a metaphor for turning away from women and abandoning marriage with them. By "committing abominations in your gatherings"—and the gathering (النادي) is the assembly where they meet, and it is not called nadiyan unless its people are in it—is meant committing the indecency or its hideous preliminaries in the sight of the group.

It is said: What is meant by "cutting off the way" is cutting off the path of those passing through their lands, for they used to perform this act upon those passing through their territory. They would throw stones at the wayfarer; whoever was hit became the "property" of the thrower, so they would take his wealth, perform the act with him, and fine him three dirhams. They had a judge who would rule thus. It is also said: Rather, they used to commit highway robbery. You have seen that the context dictates otherwise.

It is said: What is meant by "committing abominations in the gathering" is that their assemblies included various types of abominations and hideous acts, such as cursing, obscenity, gambling, throwing stones at passersby, playing stringed instruments and flutes, exposing private parts, sodomy, and the like. You have seen what the context requires.

His saying: فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ قَالُوا ائْتِنَا بِعَذَابِ اللهِ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ — "But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Bring us Allah’s punishment, if you are truthful.’" (29:29). This was mockery and derision from them. It appears from their answer that he was warning them of Allah's punishment. Allah said in his story in another place: وَلَقَدْ أَنْذَرَهُمْ بَطْشَتَنَا فَتَمَارَوْا بِالنُّذُرِ — "He had already warned them of Our punishment, but they disputed the warnings." (54:36).

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ رَبِّ انْصُرْنِي عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْمُفْسِدِينَ — "He said, ‘My Lord! Help me against the corrupt people.’" (29:30). This is a request for victory and a prayer from him against them. He counted them as corrupt (المفسدين) because of their action which corrupts the earth, cuts off progeny, and threatens humanity with extinction.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا جَاءَتْ رُسُلُنَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ بِالْبُشْرَى قَالُوا إِنَّا مُهْلِكُوا أَهْلِ هَذِهِ الْقَرْيَةِ إِنَّ أَهْلَهَا كَانُوا ظَالِمِينَ — "When Our messengers brought Abraham the good news, they said, ‘We are indeed going to destroy the people of this town. Its people are indeed wrongdoers.’" (29:31). This is a summary of the story of the destruction of Lut's people. That was through messengers from the angels whom Allah sent first to Ibrahim (peace be upon him); they gave him and his wife the good news of Ishaq and Ya‘qub, then informed him that they were sent to destroy the people of Lut. The story is detailed in Surah Hud and elsewhere.

His saying: قَالُوا إِنَّا مُهْلِكُوا أَهْلِ هَذِهِ الْقَرْيَةِ — "they said, ‘We are indeed going to destroy the people of this town." That is, they said to Ibrahim. In using the near demonstrative—"this town" (هذه القرية)—there is an indication of its proximity to the land where Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was staying, which was the Holy Land.

His saying: إِنَّ أَهْلَهَا كَانُوا ظَالِمِينَ — "Its people are indeed wrongdoers." This is a justification for their destruction on the grounds that they were wrongdoers in whom the vice of injustice had become settled. The apparent requirement was to say: "Indeed they were wrongdoers," but the explicit noun was placed instead of the pronoun to indicate that their injustice was a specific injustice belonging to them that necessitated destruction, and not just the general injustice that people were afflicted with at that time. It is as if it were said: "Indeed its people, insofar as they are its people, are wrongdoers."

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ إِنَّ فِيهَا لُوطًا قَالُوا نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ فِيهَا لَنُنَجِّيَنَّهُ وَأَهْلَهُ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَهُ كَانَتْ مِنَ الْغَابِرِينَ — "He said, ‘Lot is in it.’ They said, ‘We know better who is in it. We will surely deliver him and his family, except his wife: she shall be one of those who remain behind.’" (29:32). The appearance of the context is that he (peace be upon him) intended by his saying: إِنَّ فِيهَا لُوطًا (Lot is in it) to avert the punishment because Lut was in it and the destruction of its people would include him. So they answered him that they were not unaware of that; rather, there were others with him who would not be included in the punishment, namely his family, except his wife.

However, he (peace be upon him) was not ignorant of the fact that Allah, the Glorified, would not punish Lut while he was a sent prophet, even if the punishment included everyone else in his town; nor was he fearing for him or being terrified by His power over them. Rather, he (peace be upon him) intended by his saying: إِنَّ فِيهَا لُوطًا to avert the punishment from the people of the town out of honor for Lut, not to repel it from Lut himself. So he was answered that they were commanded to save him and take him out from among the people of the town, along with his family, except his wife who was among those remaining behind.

The proof for what we have mentioned is His saying, the Exalted, in Surah Hud at this point in the story: فَلَمَّا ذَهَبَ عَنْ إِبْرَاهِيمَ الرَّوْعُ وَجَاءَتْهُ الْبُشْرَى يُجَادِلُنَا فِي قَوْمِ لُوطٍ إِنَّ إِبْرَاهِيمَ لَحَلِيمٌ أَوَّاهٌ مُنِيبٌ يَا إِبْرَاهِيمُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هَذَا إِنَّهُ قَدْ جَاءَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ وَإِنَّهُمْ آتِيهِمْ عَذَابٌ غَيْرُ مَرْدُودٍ — "So when the awe had left Abraham and the good news had reached him, he pleaded with Us for the people of Lot. Abraham was indeed most forbearing, plaintive, [and] penitent. ‘O Abraham, let this matter alone! Your Lord’s edict has certainly come, and an irreversible punishment shall indeed overtake them.’" (11:74-76). These verses are as clear as can be that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was defending the people of Lut, not Lut himself.

Thus, the appearance of his speech (peace be upon him) in the verse we are currently discussing is a defense of Lut, and the messengers followed him in that, keeping his speech on its apparent meaning and answering that they were not ignorant of that; they knew better who was in it and were aware that Lut was in it along with his family who should not be punished, but they would save him and his family except his wife. However, what Ibrahim (peace be upon him) intended by his speech was to repel the punishment from the people of the town, so he was answered that it was a settled matter, as indicated by the verses of Surah Hud.

The commentators have long disputes regarding His saying: إِنَّ أَهْلَهَا كَانُوا ظَالِمِينَ (Its people are indeed wrongdoers) and His saying: قَالَ إِنَّ فِيهَا لُوطًا (He said, ‘Lot is in it’), which we have refrained from addressing due to lack of benefit. Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the lengthy works.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا أَنْ جَاءَتْ رُسُلُنَا لُوطًا سِيءَ بِهِمْ وَضَاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعًا وَقَالُوا لَا تَخَفْ وَلَا تَحْزَنْ — "When Our messengers came to Lot, he was distressed on their account and felt helpless for them. They said, ‘Do not be afraid, nor grieve..." (29:33) to the end of the verse. The two plural pronouns in "distressed on their account" (سيء بهم) and "helpless for them" (ضاق بهم) refer to the messengers, and the "ba" is for causality. That is, distress—which is a bad state—took hold of him because of them, and his capacity was constricted because of them, as they were in the form of handsome, beardless youths for whom he feared from the people. Then the people intended evil toward them, and Lut was too weak to repel them from them while they were guests staying in his house.

His saying: وَقَالُوا لَا تَخَفْ وَلَا تَحْزَنْ — "They said, ‘Do not be afraid, nor grieve." That is: There is no potential danger threatening you, nor any certain one befalling you. For fear (الخوف) is only regarding a possible disliked thing, and grief (الحزن) is regarding a disliked thing that has occurred.

His saying: إِنَّا مُنَجُّوكَ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَكَ كَانَتْ مِنَ الْغَابِرِينَ — "We will surely deliver you and your family, except your wife: she shall be one of those who remain behind." (29:33). That is, those remaining in the punishment. This is a justification for the negation of fear and grief.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا مُنْزِلُونَ عَلَى أَهْلِ هَذِهِ الْقَرْيَةِ رِجْزًا مِنَ السَّمَاءِ بِمَا كَانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ — "We are indeed going to bring down a punishment from the sky on the people of this town because of the transgressions they used to commit." (29:34). This is an exposition of the punishment indicated by His saying: إِنَّا مُنَجُّوكَ وَأَهْلَكَ (We will surely deliver you and your family). al-Rijz (الرجز) is punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ تَرَكْنَا مِنْهَا آيَةً بَيِّنَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ — "Certainly We have left of it a manifest sign for a people who exercise their reason." (29:35). The feminine pronoun refers to the town. "Leaving" (الترك) is preservation. That is: We have preserved from the town a clear sign for a people who exercise their reason, so they may take heed and be wary of Allah. These are the remaining ruins of it after its destruction by the descent of punishment.

Today, its location is unknown and no trace of it remains. It is sometimes said that water overwhelmed it later and it is the Sea of Lut (the Dead Sea). However, the verse is apparent—as you see—that it was visible and known at the time of the Quran's revelation. Even clearer than this is His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّهَا لَبِسَبِيلٍ مُقِيمٍ — "Indeed it is on a standing highway." (15:76) and His saying: وَإِنَّكُمْ لَتَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهِمْ مُصْبِحِينَ وَبِاللَّيْلِ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ — "Indeed you pass by them at dawn and at night. Do you not exercise your reason?" (37:137-138).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِلَى مَدْيَنَ أَخَاهُمْ شُعَيْبًا فَقَالَ يَا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَارْجُوا الْيَوْمَ الْآخِرَ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ — "And to Midian [We sent] Shu‘ayb, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah and expect the Last Day, and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption." (29:36). He calls them to the worship of Allah, which is monotheism, and to the expectation of the Last Day, which is belief in the Resurrection, and that they should not cause corruption in the earth. The primary form of their corruption therein—as mentioned in their story in other places—was the deficiency in the balance and the measure.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دَارِهِمْ جَاثِمِينَ — "But they impugned him. So the earthquake overtook them, and they lay lifeless in their homes." (29:37). The earthquake (الرجفة) is intense agitation, as mentioned by al-Raghib. al-Jathm and al-Juthum (الجثوم) in a place is sitting in it or kneeling on the ground, which is a metaphor for death. The meaning is: They impugned Shu‘ayb, so the intense agitation or the intense earthquake overtook them, and they became dead in their home, motionless.

He said in their story in another place: وَأَخَذَتِ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الصَّيْحَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دِيَارِهِمْ جَاثِمِينَ — "and the Cry overtook those who were wrongdoers, and they lay lifeless in their homes." (11:94). It is inferred from this that they were destroyed by both the Cry (الصيحة) and the Earthquake (الرجفة).

His saying, the Exalted: وَعَادًا وَثَمُودَ وَقَدْ تَبَيَّنَ لَكُمْ مِنْ مَسَاكِنِهِمْ — "And ‘Ad and Thamud, [whose fate] is indeed evident to you from their dwellings..." (29:38) to the end of the verse. He changed the context for stylistic variation (تفنناً), so He began by mentioning ‘Ad and Thamud. Likewise, in the following verse, He began by mentioning Qarun, Pharaoh, and Haman, unlike the stories of the nations mentioned previously where He began by mentioning their prophets, such as Nuh, Ibrahim, Lut, and Shu‘ayb. His saying: وَعَادًا وَثَمُودَ (‘Ad and Thamud) are in the accusative case due to an estimated verb, the estimation being: "And remember ‘Ad and Thamud."

His saying: وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ أَعْمَالَهُمْ فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ وَكَانُوا مُسْتَبْصِرِينَ — "Satan made their deeds seem glamorous to them and barred them from the way, though they used to be perceptive." (29:38). Satan's making their deeds seem glamorous to them is a borrowed metaphor (كناية استعارية) for making their evil deeds beloved to them and strengthening their attachment to them. His barring them from the way is turning them away from the way of Allah, which is the way of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being. For this reason, some have said that the meaning of their being "perceptive" (مستبصرين) is that they were previously upon the simple primordial nature.

However, it is apparent—as preceded in the commentary on His saying: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ — "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets..." (2:213)—that the era of the simple primordial nature was before the mission of Nuh (peace be upon him), and ‘Ad and Thamud were after Nuh. Thus, their being "perceptive" before being barred from the way means they were living upon the worship of Allah and the religion of monotheism, which is the religion of the primordial nature.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَارُونَ وَفِرْعَوْنَ وَهَامَانَ وَلَقَدْ جَاءَهُمْ مُوسَى بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ فَاسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَمَا كَانُوا سَابِقِينَ — "And Korah, Pharaoh, and Haman. Certainly Moses brought them manifest proofs, but they acted arrogantly in the land; yet they could not outrun [Our punishment]." (29:39). "Outrunning" (السبق) is a borrowed metaphor for prevailing (الغلبة). The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكُلًّا أَخَذْنَا بِذَنْبِهِ — "So We seized each [of them] for his sin..." (29:40) to the end of the verse. That is, each of the mentioned nations We seized for its sin. Then He began the detail, saying: فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِ حَاصِبًا — "Among them were those upon whom We unleashed a killer storm." (29:40). al-Hasib (الحاصب) refers to stones, and it is said: the wind that throws pebbles. According to the first, they are the people of Lut; according to the second, the people of ‘Ad. وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ أَخَذَتْهُ الصَّيْحَةُ — "and among them were those who were overtaken by the Cry," and they are the people of Thamud and the people of Shu‘ayb. وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ خَسَفْنَا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ — "and among them were those whom We caused the earth to swallow," and he is Qarun. وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ أَغْرَقْنَا — "and among them were those whom We drowned," and they are the people of Nuh, Pharaoh, Haman, and their people.

Then the Glorified returned to all the mentioned stories and the end to which the matter of those nations led—seizure and punishment. He explained with a general exposition that what befell them was not due to any injustice from Him, the Glorified, but rather due to their own injustice to themselves. He said: وَمَا كَانَ اللهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَلَكِنْ كَانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ — "Allah would not wrong them, but it was they who used to wrong themselves." (29:40). That is, Allah recompenses them for their injustice because the abode is an abode of trial and testing, which is the divine sunnah from which there is no deviation. So whoever is guided is guided for his own soul, and whoever goes astray, it is against it.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abu ‘Amr al-Zubayri from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): In a hadith in which he mentions the meanings of disbelief (الكفر), he said: "The fifth aspect of disbelief is the disbelief of disassociation (كفر البراءة). Allah, the Exalted, said: وَقَالَ إِنَّمَا اتَّخَذْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَوْثَانًا مَوَدَّةَ بَيْنِكُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا ثُمَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَكْفُرُ بَعْضُكُمْ بِبَعْضٍ وَيَلْعَنُ بَعْضُكُمْ بَعْضًا — meaning some of you shall disassociate from others." — the hadith.

I say: This meaning was also narrated in al-Tawhid from ‘Ali (peace be upon him) in a long hadith in which he answers what he was asked regarding the [apparent] inconsistency of verses. Therein: "And disbelief (الكفر) in this verse is disassociation (البراءة); He says: some of them shall disassociate from others. Its parallel in Surah Ibrahim is the saying of Satan: إِنِّي كَفَرْتُ بِمَا أَشْرَكْتُمُونِ مِنْ قَبْلُ — 'I disown your having pleaded me as a partner [with Allah] before' (14:22), and the saying of Ibrahim, the Friend of the All-Beneficent: كَفَرْنَا بِكُمْ — 'We disown you' (60:4), meaning: we disassociate from you."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Jabir: That the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) forbade al-khadhf (throwing pebbles), and it is the saying of Allah: وَتَأْتُونَ فِي نَادِيكُمُ الْمُنْكَرَ — "and commit abominations in your gatherings."

I say: This meaning was also narrated from several authors of the Jawami‘ from Umm Hani bint Abi Talib. The wording of the hadith is: She said: "I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about the saying of Allah: وَتَأْتُونَ فِي نَادِيكُمُ الْمُنْكَرَ. He said: 'They used to sit by the road, throw pebbles at the wayfarer, and mock them.'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abu Zayd al-Hammad from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): In a hadith regarding the descent of the angels upon Ibrahim with the good news, he said: "Ibrahim said to them: 'Why have you come?' They said: 'For the destruction of the people of Lut.' He said to them: 'If there are a hundred believers in it, will you destroy them?' Jibra’il said: 'No.' He said: 'If there are fifty?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'If there are thirty?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'If there are twenty?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'If there are ten?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'If there are five?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'If there is one?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'Indeed Lut is in it.' They said: 'We know better who is in it. We will surely deliver him and his family, except his wife: she shall be one of those who remain behind.'"

Al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali (peace be upon him) said: "I do not know this saying except that he was trying to spare them, and it is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يُجَادِلُنَا فِي قَوْمِ لُوطٍ — 'he pleaded with Us for the people of Lot.'"

Ayahs

مَثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْلِيَآءَ كَمَثَلِ ٱلْعَنكَبُوتِ ٱتَّخَذَتْ بَيْتًا ۖ وَإِنَّ أَوْهَنَ ٱلْبُيُوتِ لَبَيْتُ ٱلْعَنكَبُوتِ ۖ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (٤١)
The parable of those who take guardians instead of Allah is that of the spider that takes a home, and indeed the frailest of homes is the home of a spider, had they known! (41)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٤٢)
Allah indeed knows whatever thing they invoke besides Him, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (42)
وَتِلْكَ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ ۖ وَمَا يَعْقِلُهَآ إِلَّا ٱلْعَـٰلِمُونَ (٤٣)
We draw these parables for mankind; but no one grasps them except those who have knowledge. (43)
خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٤٤)
Allah created the heavens and the earth with consummate wisdom. There is indeed a sign in that for the faithful. (44)
ٱتْلُ مَآ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَأَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ تَنْهَىٰ عَنِ ٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَٱلْمُنكَرِ ۗ وَلَذِكْرُ ٱللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تَصْنَعُونَ (٤٥)
Recite what has been revealed to you of the Book, and maintain the prayer. Indeed the prayer restrains from indecent and wrongful conduct, and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater. And Allah knows whatever [deeds] you do. (45)
۞ وَلَا تُجَـٰدِلُوٓا۟ أَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِلَّا بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ ۖ وَقُولُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْنَا وَأُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ وَإِلَـٰهُنَا وَإِلَـٰهُكُمْ وَٰحِدٌ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ (٤٦)
Do not argue with the People of the Book except in a manner which is best, except such of them as are wrongdoers, and say, ‘We believe in what has been sent down to us and in what has been sent down to you; our God and your God is one [and the same], and to Him do we submit.’ (46)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ۚ فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۖ وَمِنْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِهِۦ ۚ وَمَا يَجْحَدُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِلَّا ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٤٧)
Thus We have sent down the Book to you; those to whom We have given the Book believe in it, and of these there are some who believe in it, and none contests Our signs except the faithless. (47)
وَمَا كُنتَ تَتْلُوا۟ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ مِن كِتَـٰبٍ وَلَا تَخُطُّهُۥ بِيَمِينِكَ ۖ إِذًا لَّٱرْتَابَ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ (٤٨)
You did not use to recite any scripture before it, nor did you write it with your right hand, for then the impugners would have been skeptical. (48)
بَلْ هُوَ ءَايَـٰتٌۢ بَيِّنَـٰتٌ فِى صُدُورِ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ ۚ وَمَا يَجْحَدُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِلَّا ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٤٩)
Indeed, it is [present as] manifest signs in the breasts of those who have been given knowledge, and none contests Our signs except wrongdoers. (49)
وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَـٰتٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۖ قُلْ إِنَّمَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَإِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٥٠)
They say, ‘Why has not some sign been sent down to him from his Lord?’ Say, ‘These signs are only from Allah, and I am only a manifest warner.’ (50)
أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِهِمْ أَنَّآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَرَحْمَةً وَذِكْرَىٰ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥١)
Does it not suffice them that We have sent down to you the Book which is recited to them? There is indeed in that a mercy and admonition for a people who have faith. (51)
قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ شَهِيدًا ۖ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ وَكَفَرُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (٥٢)
Say, ‘Allah suffices as a witness between me and you: He knows whatever there is in the heavens and the earth. Those who put faith in falsehood and defy Allah—it is they who are the losers.’ (52)
وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِٱلْعَذَابِ ۚ وَلَوْلَآ أَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى لَّجَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ وَلَيَأْتِيَنَّهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٥٣)
They ask you to hasten the punishment. Yet were it not for a specified time, the punishment would have surely overtaken them. Surely, it will overtake them suddenly while they are unaware. (53)
يَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِٱلْعَذَابِ وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌۢ بِٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٥٤)
They ask you to hasten the punishment, and indeed hell will besiege the faithless (54)
يَوْمَ يَغْشَىٰهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ مِن فَوْقِهِمْ وَمِن تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِهِمْ وَيَقُولُ ذُوقُوا۟ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٥٥)
on the day when the punishment envelopes them, from above them and from under their feet, and He will say, ‘Taste what you used to do!’ (55)

(Commentary)

The verses include a concluding reflection on the stories of those past perished nations, presented through a parable struck by Allah, the Glorified, regarding their taking of guardians (أولياء) besides Allah. In it, He explains that their construction is the frailest of constructions; the very creation of the heavens and the earth cries out its falsehood and corruption, and that they have no guardian besides Him, as this Book mentions.

From here, the discourse transitions to commanding the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to recite this Book which has been revealed to him, to maintain the prayer (إقامة الصلاة), and to invite the People of the Book with gentle speech and fair disputation. It also responds to the proposal of the polytheists to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he should bring them signs other than the Quran, and their demand that he hasten the punishment with which he warns them.

His saying, the Exalted: مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَوْلِياءَ كَمَثَلِ الْعَنْكَبُوتِ اتَّخَذَتْ بَيْتاً — The parable of those who take guardians besides Allah is that of the spider who builds a house (29:41), to the end of the verse. The spider (العنكبوت) is well-known; the term is used for both the singular and the collective, and it can be treated as masculine or feminine.

The focus in His saying, "The parable of those who take..." is on the act of taking guardians besides Allah; hence the use of the relative pronoun and its clause. Similarly, the focus in His saying, "is that of the spider who builds a house," is on its building of the house. Thus, the meaning resolves to: the state of the polytheists in their taking of guardians besides Allah is like the state of the spider in its building of a house that has a particular report—which is the description indicated by the indefiniteness of "a house" (بَيْتاً).

His saying, إِنَّ أَوْهَنَ الْبُيُوتِ لَبَيْتُ الْعَنْكَبُوتِ — Indeed the frailest of houses is the spider’s house (29:41), serves as an explanation of the quality of the house the spider has taken. He did not say, "Indeed the frailest of houses is its house," as the literal context might suggest, in order to treat the sentence as a proverbial maxim (المثل السائر) that remains unchanged.

The meaning is: their taking of guardians besides Allah—who are their deities whom they follow and rely upon—is like the spider’s taking of a house, which is the frailest of houses. It possesses nothing of the characteristics of a house except the name; it repels neither heat nor cold, provides no shelter for a person, and protects against no harm. Likewise, the guardianship (ولاية) of their guardians is nothing but a name; they neither benefit nor harm, nor do they possess power over death, life, or resurrection (النشور).

The specific instance of the parable is the polytheists taking deities besides Allah. The substitution of "deities" with "guardians" (الأولياء) is because the motive driving them to take these deities was their claim that these deities possessed guardianship over their affairs and the governance (تدبير) of their state, in terms of bringing good to them, repelling evil from them, and interceding on their behalf.

The verse—in addition to fulfilling this subtle point—includes in its generality everyone who, in any matter or affair, takes a guardian besides Allah, relying upon him and viewing him as independent in the effect hoped for from him, even if he is not counted among the idols—unless his guardianship traces back to the guardianship of Allah, such as the guardianship of the Messenger, the Imams, and the believers. As the Exalted said: وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ — And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him (12:106).

His saying, لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ — if they knew (29:41), means: if they knew that their parable is like that of the spider, they would not have taken them as guardians. So it has been said.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ يَعْلَمُ ما يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ — Indeed Allah knows whatever thing they invoke besides Him, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise (29:42). It is possible for "what" (ما) in "whatever they invoke" to be a relative pronoun, a negative particle, an interrogative, or an infinitive-forming particle. The "from" (مِنْ) in "from any thing" (مِنْ شَيْءٍ) would be redundant for emphasis according to the second possibility, and for clarification (للتبيين) according to the others. The most likely possibilities are the first two, and the most preferred of them is the first.

The meaning according to the second possibility is that Allah knows they are not invoking anything besides Him; i.e., the deities they worship have no reality. Thus, as the author of al-Kashshaf stated, it would be an emphasis of the parable and an addition to it, as He does not even consider what they invoke to be a "thing."

The meaning according to the first possibility is that Allah knows the thing they invoke besides Him and is not ignorant of it. This would be an allusion (كناية) to the fact that the parable He struck is appropriate, and that their guardians possess nothing of guardianship except the name.

This meaning is reinforced by the two Noble Names: the All-mighty (العزيز) and the All-wise (الحكيم) at the end of the verse. For He, the Exalted, is the All-mighty (العزيز) — the One Whom nothing overcomes, so no one shares with Him in the governance of His kingdom, just as no one shares with Him in creation and origination. He is the All-wise (الحكيم) — the One Who performs every act and governance with absolute precision, so He does not delegate the governance of His creation to anyone. This serves as a preamble for what will be explained in His saying: خَلَقَ اللهُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِ — Allah created the heavens and the earth with the truth (29:44).

His saying, the Exalted: وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثالُ نَضْرِبُها لِلنَّاسِ وَما يَعْقِلُها إِلَّا الْعالِمُونَ — And these parables We strike for mankind, but none grasps them except the learned (29:43). This indicates that while the parables struck in the Quran are general in that they reach the ears of all people, the insight into the reality of their meanings and the core of their objectives is exclusive to the people of knowledge—those who grasp the realities of matters and do not remain frozen at their outward appearances.

The evidence for this meaning is His saying, "but none grasps them" (وَما يَعْقِلُها), rather than saying, "but none believes in them," or something similar.

Thus, people differ in their reception of the parables struck in His speech according to the variation in their understanding. There is the listener who gains nothing from them except the reception of the words and the conceptualization of their simple meanings without deep reflection or probing their depths. And there is the listener who receives with his hearing what those others hear, but then dives into their deep objectives and grasps their elegant realities.

In this is a notification that the comparison of their taking guardians besides Allah to the spider’s taking of a house—which is the frailest of houses—is not merely a poetic metaphor or a claim devoid of evidence. Rather, it rests upon a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية) and a true, established reality, which is what the following verse points toward.

His saying, the Exalted: خَلَقَ اللهُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ — Allah created the heavens and the earth with the truth. Indeed in that is a sign for the believers (29:44). What is meant by the creation of the heavens and the earth being "with the truth" (بِالْحَقِّ) is the negation of play (اللعب) in their creation, as the Exalted said: وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَاعِبِينَ مَا خَلَقْنَاهُمَا إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ — We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with the truth, but most of them do not know (44:38-39).

The creation of the heavens and the earth is based on a stable, unchanging system and a continuous divine sunnah (سنة إلهية) that does not vary or fail. Creation (الخلق) and governance (التدبير) do not differ in reality, nor is one separable from the other (1). Since creation and making trace back to Him, the Exalted, by a necessary tracing from which there is no escape, then governance also belongs to Him, and there is no escape from that. There is nothing other than Him, the Exalted, except that it is His creature, sustained by Him, and owned by Him; it possesses for itself neither benefit nor harm. It is impossible for it to undertake any part of the governance of its own affair or the affair of another in a way that it is independent and self-sufficient in its matter apart from Him, the Exalted. This is the truth (الحق) in which there is no play, and the seriousness (الجد) in which there is no jest.

Thus, when some of His creatures took charge of the affairs of others, that was not a true guardianship (ولاية حق) on their part, because they possess nothing in the true sense of ownership (الملك). Rather, that action of theirs was based on play, and for Him, the Exalted, to delegate the matter of governance to them would be play on His part—exalted and sanctified is He—since it is nothing but a supposition with no reality and an illusion with no factuality; and that is the meaning of play.

From this, it becomes clear that the guardianship of those whose guardianship they invoke has nothing but the name of guardianship without the named reality, just as the spider’s house is likewise.

His saying, إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ — Indeed in that is a sign for the believers (29:44), specifies the believers for mention, despite the sign being general to them and others, because those who benefit from it are the believers and none else.

His saying, the Exalted: اتْلُ ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ إِنَّ الصَّلاةَ تَنْهى عَنِ الْفَحْشاءِ وَالْمُنْكَرِ وَلَذِكْرُ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ — Recite what has been revealed to you of the Book, and maintain the prayer. Indeed the prayer prevents indecency and wrongdoing, and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater (29:45), etc. When He mentioned the summary of the stories of the nations and the inevitable misery and permanent loss that their polytheism and commission of indecency and wrongdoing led to, He transitioned from that—resuming the discourse—to commanding him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to recite what was revealed to him of the Book. This is because it is the best deterrent against polytheism and the commission of indecency and wrongdoing, due to the clear signs it contains, which include luminous proofs for the truth, stories, lessons, exhortations, glad tidings, warnings, promises, and threats. By the recitation of its verses, both the reciter and the listener are deterred.

He coupled this with the command to maintain the prayer, which is the best of deeds, and justified it by saying: "Indeed the prayer prevents indecency and wrongdoing." The context bears witness that what is meant by this prevention is the deterrence of the nature of the act (طبيعة العمل) from indecency and wrongdoing by way of requirement (الاقتضاء) rather than total causality (العلية التامة).

For the nature of this devotional turning—when the servant performs it while repeating it five times every day and persevering in it, especially if he practices it within a righteous society where others perform the like and care for what he cares for—is to deter him from every major sin that the religious taste finds loathsome, such as the wrongful killing of a soul, the unjust consumption of the orphan's wealth, adultery, and sodomy, and from everything that the sound nature and upright primordial nature (الفطرة) reject—a deterrence that combines instruction and action.

This is because it first instructs him, through the remembrance (الذكر) it contains, in the belief in His oneness, the message, and the recompense of the Day of Judgment; and that he should address his Lord with sincerity of worship, seeking His aid, and asking for guidance to His straight path (الصراط المستقيم), seeking refuge from His wrath and from straying. Secondly, it compels him to turn with his soul and body toward the courtyard of Grandeur and Majesty, remembering his Lord with praise, commendation, glorification, and magnification, then offering peace upon himself, his peers, and all the righteous servants of Allah.

In addition, it compels him to purify himself from ritual and physical impurity in his body, to be pure in his clothing, to avoid usurpation in his garments and place, and to face the House of his Lord. If a human being were to persevere in his prayer for even a short period and employ some degree of sincerity in its performance, that would certainly establish in his soul a habit (ملكة) of deterring from indecency and wrongdoing. If you were to appoint over yourself someone to raise you with a righteous upbringing by which you would become fit for this station and adorned with the etiquette of servitude, he would not command you with more than what the prayer commands you, nor would he discipline you with more than what the prayer disciplines you.

An objection has been raised against the verse: we often find among those who pray those who do not care about committing major sins and are not deterred from wrongdoings; thus, their prayer does not prevent them from indecency and wrongdoing.

Therefore, some have mentioned that "prayer" in the verse means "supplication" (الدعاء), and what is meant is the call to the command of Allah. The meaning would be: establish the call to the command of Allah, for that deters people from indecency and wrongdoing. This is rejected as it turns the speech away from its outward meaning.

Others mentioned that "prayer" in the verse is used in an indefinite sense; the meaning being that some types of prayer or some individual instances of it necessitate the cessation of indecency and wrongdoing. This is true, and the meaning is not exhaustive (الاستغراق) such that the objection would apply.

A group mentioned that its prevention of indecency and wrongdoing lasts as long as it is being performed and the person is in his prayer. It is as if it were said: the person praying, as long as he is praying, is occupied away from disobeying Allah by committing indecency and wrongdoing.

Some said: the verse is according to its outward meaning, and the prayer is like one who forbids and says: "Do not do such-and-such and do not commit such-and-such," but the prohibition does not necessitate the cessation. The prohibition of the prayer is not greater than His own prohibition, as in His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسانِ وَإِيتاءِ ذِي الْقُرْبى وَيَنْهى عَنِ الْفَحْشاءِ وَالْمُنْكَرِ — Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness and generosity towards relatives, and He forbids indecency, wrongdoing, and aggression (16:90). His prohibition does not necessitate cessation, and the objection is only built upon the illusion that prohibition entails cessation, which is a false illusion.

It is narrated from some in repelling the objection that the prayer is established for the remembrance of Allah, as the Exalted said: أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِذِكْرِي — maintain the prayer for My remembrance (20:14). Whoever is remembering Allah, that prevents him from doing what He dislikes. Everyone you see who prays yet commits indecency and wrongdoing is such that if he did not pray, he would be even more prone to committing them; thus, the prayer has had an effect in reducing his indecency and wrongdoing.

You are well aware that none of these answers harmonize with the context of the ruling and the justification in the verse. For what the context provides is that the command to maintain the prayer was justified by His saying: "Indeed the prayer prevents indecency and wrongdoing," to indicate that the prayer is a devotional act whose performance bequeaths a spiritual quality in the human being that acts as a deterrent for him against indecency and wrongdoing. Thus, the soul is refined from indecency and wrongdoing and purified from the filth of sins and offenses.

So what is meant is the seeking of the habit (ملكة) of deterrence, which is among the effects of the nature of prayer by way of requirement (الاقتضاء), not that it is the effect of only some individual instances of the nature of prayer as in the second answer; nor that it is the effect of being occupied with the prayer only as long as one is occupied with it as in the third answer; nor that what is meant is the seeking of the reception of the prayer's prohibition alone without regard for the cessation from its prohibition—as if it were said: "maintain the prayer so that you may hear its prohibition"—as in the fourth answer; nor that what is meant is: "maintain the prayer so that the remembrance it contains may prevent you from indecency and wrongdoing" as in the fifth answer.

The truth in the answer is that deterrence is an effect of the nature of prayer, which is a specific devotional turning toward Allah, the Glorified. This is by way of requirement (الاقتضاء) rather than necessity and total causality. It may sometimes fail to produce its effect due to the presence of certain obstacles that weaken the remembrance and bring it closer to heedlessness and diversion from the core of remembrance. Whenever the remembrance (الذكر) strengthens, and presence and humility (الخشوع) become complete, and sincerity (الإخلاص) becomes pure, the effect of deterrence from indecency and wrongdoing increases; and whenever it weakens, the effect weakens.

If you reflect on the state of some of those among the people who are called Muslims but have abandoned the prayer, you will find that by abandoning the prayer, they lose the obligation of fasting, pilgrimage, zakat, khums, and all religious duties. They do not distinguish between the pure and the impure, or the lawful and the unlawful; they go their own way, paying no heed to anything. Then, if you compare to them the state of one who performs the lowest levels of prayer by which the obligation is discharged, you will find him deterred from much of what the one who abandons prayer commits without concern. Then, if you compare to him one who is above him in concern for the matter of prayer, you will find him even more deterred than him, and so on.

His saying, وَلَذِكْرُ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ — and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater (29:45). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: Remembrance (الذكر) is sometimes said to mean a state of the soul by which a human being can preserve the knowledge he acquires; it is like "preservation" (الحفظ), except that preservation is said in consideration of its being stored, and remembrance is said in consideration of its being summoned. Sometimes it is said for the presence of a thing in the heart or the speech. Therefore, it is said: remembrance is of two types: remembrance after forgetting, and remembrance not after forgetting but from continuous preservation. Every speech is also called remembrance. (End of quote).

It appears that the origin of its meaning is the first sense. Calling the word "remembrance" is only because it contains the heart-based meaning. Heart-based remembrance (الذكر القلبي) in relation to the verbal is like the effect resulting from its cause and the objective intended by the act.

The prayer is called remembrance because it contains verbal remembrances (الأذكار القولية) such as the declaration of oneness (تهليل), praise (تحميد), and glorification (تنزيه). From another perspective, it is one of the instances of remembrance because, in its totality, it is a representation of the servant's servitude to Allah, the Glorified, as He said: إِذا نُودِيَ لِلصَّلاةِ مِنْ يَوْمِ الْجُمُعَةِ فَاسْعَوْا إِلى ذِكْرِ اللهِ — when the call is made for prayer on Friday, hasten to the remembrance of Allah (62:9). From yet another perspective, it is a matter upon which remembrance follows as an objective follows its means, as indicated by His saying: وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِذِكْرِي — and maintain the prayer for My remembrance (20:14).

The remembrance which is the objective resulting from the prayer—I mean heart-based remembrance in the sense of the presence of the Remembered in the faculty of perception after its absence through forgetfulness, or the continuation of its presence—is the best deed whose issuance from a human being can be conceived; it is the highest in rank and the greatest in value and effect. For it is the ultimate felicity prepared for the human being and the key to every good.

Furthermore, it appears from the context of His saying, "and maintain the prayer; indeed the prayer prevents indecency and wrongdoing," that His saying, "and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater," is connected to it, explaining another effect of the prayer that is greater than what was explained before it. Thus, His saying, "and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater," takes the place of a transition to a higher point (الإضراب والترقي). The intended meaning would be the heart-based remembrance that follows the prayer as an objective follows its means. It is as if it were said: maintain the prayer so that it may deter you from indecency and wrongdoing; rather, the remembrance of Allah that you gain through it is greater than that—i.e., than the prevention of indecency and wrongdoing—because it is the greatest good a human being can attain and the key to every good, while the prevention of indecency and wrongdoing is only part of the good.

It is also possible that "remembrance" refers to the remembrance contained within the prayer or the prayer itself. The sentence would also be a transition to a higher point, and the meaning: rather, the remembrance of Allah contained in the prayer, or the prayer itself which is the remembrance of Allah, is greater than this effect which is the prevention of indecency and wrongdoing, because the prevention is but one of its beautiful effects. According to both possibilities, "the remembrance of Allah" (لَذِكْرُ اللهِ) is an infinitive added to its object, and that which it is "greater" (أَكْبَرُ) than is the prevention of indecency and wrongdoing.

Regarding the meaning of "remembrance," whether the word it is added to is the subject or the object of the infinitive, and whether that which it is "greater" than is specific or general, they have other opinions.

It was said: the meaning of the verse is: Allah’s remembrance of the servant is greater than the servant’s remembrance of Allah. This is because Allah remembers the one who remembers Him, due to His saying: فَاذْكُرُونِي أَذْكُرْكُمْ — So remember Me, and I will remember you (2:152). It was said: the meaning is: Allah’s remembrance of the servant is greater than the prayer. It was said: the meaning is: Allah’s remembrance of the servant is greater than everything.

It was said: the meaning is: the servant’s remembrance of Allah in the prayer is greater than the other pillars of the prayer. It was said: the meaning is: the servant’s remembrance of Allah in the prayer is greater than his remembrance of Him outside the prayer. It was said: the meaning is: the servant’s remembrance of Allah is greater than all his other deeds. It was said: the meaning is: the prayer is greater than all other acts of obedience. It was said: the meaning is: the servant’s remembrance of Allah at the time of indecency and wrongdoing and his remembrance of His prohibition of them is greater than the restraint and deterrence of the prayer. It was said: the word "greater" (أَكْبَرُ) is stripped of the meaning of comparison and does not require something to be compared to, like His saying: ما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ مِنَ اللهْوِ — what is with Allah is better than diversion (62:11).

These are their scattered opinions, which we have overlooked investigating for the sake of brevity. Reflection on the verse is sufficient to spare the trouble of investigation, especially since the arbitrary nature of some of them is clear and not hidden.

His saying, وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تَصْنَعُونَ — And Allah knows what you do (29:45), means: whatever you do of good or evil. So you must be mindful of Him and not be heedless of Him. In this is an urging and incitement toward mindfulness (المراقبة), especially according to the first interpretation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تُجادِلُوا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْهُمْ — Do not dispute with the People of the Book except in a manner which is best, barring such of them as are wrongdoers (29:46). When He commanded in His saying, "Recite what has been revealed to you..." etc., the delivery of the message and the invitation through the recitation of the Book, He followed it with an explanation of the manner of invitation. He forbade disputing with the People of the Book—who, according to what the generality requires, are the Jews and the Christians, and with them are joined the Magians and the Sabians—except with the disputation (المجادلة) that is the best of disputation.

Disputation is only "best" when it does not include harshness, slander, or insult. Part of its being "best" is that it be coupled with gentleness and softness in speech so that the opponent is not pained, and that the disputant draws near to his opponent and approaches him until they agree and cooperate to manifest the truth without obstinacy or stubbornness. When it combines softness of speech and an approach of agreement, its beauty increases further, becoming the "best."

For this reason, when He forbade disputing with them except in the manner which is best, He excepted from it "those who are wrongdoers among them." For what is meant by "wrongdoing" (الظلم), by the context of the passage, is the opponent being such that gentleness, softness, and an approach toward the objective do not benefit him; rather, he receives the fair disputation as a kind of humiliation and lowliness for the disputant, and considers it a deception and a trick to turn him away from his belief. With these wrongdoers, disputation in the best manner does not succeed.

For this reason also, He followed the speech with an explanation of the manner of approaching them and building the disputation upon a word in which both opponents meet, so they draw near to each other and cooperate in the manifestation of the truth. He said: وَقُولُوا آمَنَّا بِالَّذِي أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْنا وَأُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ وَإِلهُنا وَإِلهُكُمْ واحِدٌ وَنَحْنُ لَهُ مُسْلِمُونَ — and say, ‘We believe in what has been sent down to us and was sent down to you. Our God and your God is one, and to Him do we submit’ (29:46). The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ فَالَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ وَمِنْ هؤُلاءِ مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِهِ وَما يَجْحَدُ بِآياتِنا إِلَّا الْكافِرُونَ — Thus have We sent down the Book to you; so those to whom We have given the Book believe in it, and of these there are some who believe in it, and none denies Our signs except the faithless (29:47). That is, according to that quality—which is submission (الإسلام) to Allah and the confirmation of His books and messengers—We sent down the Quran to you.

It was said: the meaning is: like the Book We sent down to Moses and Jesus, We sent down the Book to you, which is the Quran.

His saying, "so those to whom We have given the Book..." etc., is a derivation from the manner of the Book's revelation. That is, since the Quran was revealed in submission to Allah and confirmation of His books and messengers, the People of the Book believe in it according to their nature, because of the belief they have in Allah and the confirmation of His books and messengers. And "of these"—meaning the polytheists from the idol-worshippers—there are those who believe in it. And none denies Our signs or rejects them from among the People of the Book and these polytheists except the faithless (الكافرون), who are those who cover the truth with falsehood.

It has been considered that what is meant by "those to whom We have given the Book" are the Muslims, and the one pointed to by "these" are the People of the Book, but this is far-fetched. Equally far-fetched is returning the pronoun in "believe in it" to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In His saying, "and of these there are some who believe in it," there is a kind of independent recognition for those among the polytheists who believed in it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كُنْتَ تَتْلُوا مِنْ قَبْلِهِ مِنْ كِتابٍ وَلا تَخُطُّهُ بِيَمِينِكَ إِذاً لَارْتابَ الْمُبْطِلُونَ — You did not use to recite any scripture before it, nor did you write it with your right hand, for then the purveyors of falsehood would have had doubts (29:48). Recitation (التلاوة) is reading, whether from memory or from a written book; what is meant here is the second, by the context of the situation. Writing (الخط) is the act of inscribing. The purveyors of falsehood (المبطلون) is the plural of mubtil, which is one who brings falsehood in speech; it is also said for one who seeks to nullify the truth, i.e., claims its falsehood. The second meaning is more appropriate in the verse, though the first is also possible.

The outward expression in His saying, "You did not use to recite..." etc., is the negation of habit; i.e., it was not your habit to recite and write, as indicated by His saying in another place: فَقَدْ لَبِثْتُ فِيكُمْ عُمُراً مِنْ قَبْلِهِ — I have already lived among you a lifetime before it (10:16).

It was said that what is meant is the negation of ability; i.e., you were not able to recite and write before it. The first view is more appropriate regarding the context of the proof, which He established to confirm the truth of the Quran and its revelation from Him.

The qualification of His saying, "nor did you write it," with His saying, "with your right hand" (بِيَمِينِكَ), is a type of representation that provides emphasis, like one saying: "I saw it with my own eyes" and "I heard it with my own ears."

The meaning is: it was not your habit before the revelation of the Quran to read a book, nor was it your habit to write a book and inscribe it—meaning you did not master reading and writing because you were unlettered (أمي)—and had it been so, these purveyors of falsehood, who nullify the truth by claiming it is false, would have doubted. But since you did not master reading and writing and continued in that state, and they knew you in this condition due to your mixing and living with them, there remained no room for doubt for them regarding the matter of the Quran revealed to you—that it is the speech of Allah and not a fabrication you composed from the books of the predecessors or copied from their stories and others—such that the purveyors of falsehood would doubt and use it as an excuse.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ هُوَ آياتٌ بَيِّناتٌ فِي صُدُورِ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَما يَجْحَدُ بِآياتِنا إِلَّا الظَّالِمُونَ — Rather it is [a set of] clear signs in the breasts of those who have been given knowledge, and none denies Our signs except the wrongdoers (29:49). This is a transition (إضراب) from an implied point understood from the previous verse. It is as if when He negated for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) both recitation and writing, it resulted from that that the Quran is not a composed, written book. So He transitioned from this implied point by His saying: "Rather it—i.e., the Quran—is clear signs in the breasts of those who have been given knowledge."

His saying, وَما يَجْحَدُ بِآياتِنا إِلَّا الظَّالِمُونَ — and none denies Our signs except the wrongdoers (29:49). What is meant by "wrongdoing" (الظلم), by the context of the situation, is wrongdoing against the signs of Allah by denying them and being arrogant against accepting them out of obstinacy and stubbornness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آياتٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ قُلْ إِنَّمَا الْآياتُ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَإِنَّما أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ — They say, ‘Why have not some signs been sent down to him from his Lord?’ Say, ‘The signs are only with Allah, and I am only a manifest warner’ (29:50). When He mentioned the Book and commanded the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to recite it and invite them to it, and that some of them believe in it while others do not—who are the faithless wrongdoers—He pointed in this verse and the two verses after it to their lack of regard for the Quran, which is the sign of prophethood, and their proposal to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he should bring them signs other than it, and the response to that.

So His saying, "They say, ‘Why have not some signs been sent down to him from his Lord?’" is a proposal from them to bring signs other than the Quran, as an insinuation from them that it is not a sign, and based on their claim that a prophet must possess a divine, unseen power by which he is capable of whatever he wishes. In their saying, "Why have not [some signs] been sent down to him," rather than saying, "Why does he not bring us signs," there is a type of mockery, like their saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ لَوْ مَا تَأْتِينا بِالْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ — O you to whom the Reminder has been sent down, you are indeed crazy! Why do you not bring us the angels, if you are truthful? (15:6-7).

His saying, قُلْ إِنَّمَا الْآياتُ عِنْدَ اللهِ — Say, ‘The signs are only with Allah’ (29:50), is a response to their claim that whoever claims a message claims an unseen power by which he is capable of whatever he wishes. The response is that the signs are with Allah; He sends them down whenever He wishes and however He wills; no one shares with Him in the power over them. Thus, nothing is up to the Prophet except if Allah wills. Then He added further explanation by restricting the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) role to warning only, by His saying: "and I am only a manifest warner."

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِهِمْ أَنَّا أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ يُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ — Does it not suffice them that We have sent down to you the Book which is recited to them? (29:51), to the end of the verse, is a preparation and preamble for the response to their insinuation against the Quran that it is not a sign. The interrogation is for denial (الإنكار) and the address is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). That is: does it not suffice them as a sign, this Book which We have sent down to you, while it is recited to them, so they hear it and know its station of inimitability (الإعجاز), and it is full of mercy and a reminder for the believers?

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ كَفى بِاللهِ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ شَهِيداً — Say, ‘Allah suffices as a witness between me and you’ (29:52). This is a response given to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) so that he may answer them with it: that Allah, the Glorified, is a witness between me and you in what we dispute over, which is the matter of the message. For He, the Glorified, bears witness in His speech which He sent down to me to my message, and He, the Exalted, knows what is in the heavens and the earth without being ignorant of anything; and His witness for me suffices as a proof for my claim.

They have no right to say that it is not the speech of Allah, because of His challenge (تحديه) time after time within the verses. From this, it is known that His saying, "Say, ‘Allah suffices as a witness between me and you’" is not a mere claim or rhetorical speech, but rather a demonstrative explanation and a definitive proof, as you have seen.

His saying, وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْباطِلِ وَكَفَرُوا بِاللهِ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ — Those who believe in falsehood and defy Allah—it is they who are the losers (29:52). Loss is restricted to them because of their lack of belief in Allah through disbelief in His Book, which contains His testimony to the message. By their disbelief in Allah, the Truth, they believe in falsehood, and therefore they lost in their belief.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِالْعَذابِ وَلَوْ لا أَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى لَجاءَهُمُ الْعَذابُ وَلَيَأْتِيَنَّهُمْ بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ — They bid you to hasten the punishment. Had there not been a specified time, the punishment would have surely come to them; and it will surely overtake them suddenly while they are unaware (29:53). This is an allusion to their saying, like the saying of their predecessors: "Bring us the punishment of Allah if you are of the truthful." Allah has narrated their seeking to hasten in His saying: وَلَئِنْ أَخَّرْنَا عَنْهُمُ الْعَذَابَ إِلَى أُمَّةٍ مَعْدُودَةٍ لَيَقُولُنَّ مَا يَحْبِسُهُ — And if We should delay the punishment from them until a reckoned time, they will surely say, ‘What detains it?’ (11:8).

What is meant by the "specified time" (الأجل المسمى) is that which He decreed for the children of Adam when He sent Adam down to the earth, saying: وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ — and there will be for you on the earth an abode and a provision for a while (2:36), and He said: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ ساعَةً وَلا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ — Every nation has a term; when their term comes, they shall not put it back by a single hour, nor shall they put it forward (7:34).

This punishment, which the specified time stands between them and it, is that which they deserve for their evil deeds in general, as the Mightiest of speakers said: وَرَبُّكَ الْغَفُورُ ذُو الرَّحْمَةِ لَوْ يُؤاخِذُهُمْ بِما كَسَبُوا لَعَجَّلَ لَهُمُ الْعَذابَ بَلْ لَهُمْ مَوْعِدٌ لَنْ يَجِدُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ مَوْئِلاً — Your Lord is the All-forgiving, possessor of mercy. Were He to take them to task for what they have earned, He would have surely hastened the punishment for them. But they have a tryst from which they will not find any refuge (18:58). This does not contradict the hastening of punishment through the descent of the signs proposed to the Messenger without delay or respite. The Exalted said: وَمَا مَنَعَنَا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآيَاتِ إِلَّا أَنْ كَذَّبَ بِهَا الْأَوَّلُونَ — Nothing prevented Us from sending the signs except that the former peoples denied them (17:59).

His saying, the Exalted: يَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِالْعَذابِ وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ ، يَوْمَ يَغْشاهُمُ الْعَذابُ — They bid you to hasten the punishment, and indeed hell will surely beset the faithless, on the day when the punishment shall envelop them (29:54-55), to the end of the verse. The repetition of "They bid you to hasten" is to indicate the completeness of their ignorance and the corruption of their understanding; and that their seeking to hasten is, firstly, a seeking to hasten a matter that is deferred, not immediate; and secondly, a seeking to hasten a punishment that is already occurring and from which there is no turning away, because they are being recompensed by their deeds which do not leave them.

Enveloping (الغشاوة والغشاية) is covering in the sense of surrounding. His saying, "on the day when [the punishment] shall envelop them," is an adverbial phrase for "will surely beset." The rest is clear.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَما يَعْقِلُها إِلَّا الْعالِمُونَ — but none grasps them except the learned (29:43), al-Wahidi narrated with a chain of transmission from Jabir, who said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited this verse and said: 'The learned (العالم) is he who grasps from Allah—so he acts in His obedience and avoids His wrath.'"

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الصَّلاةَ تَنْهى عَنِ الْفَحْشاءِ وَالْمُنْكَرِ — Indeed the prayer prevents indecency and wrongdoing (29:45), Anas ibn Malik narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): من لم تنهه صلاته عن الفحشاء والمنكر لم يزدد من الله إلا بعدا — "Whoever's prayer does not prevent him from indecency and wrongdoing, he only increases in distance from Allah."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from ‘Imran ibn al-Husayn, Ibn Mas‘ud, Ibn ‘Abbas, and Ibn ‘Umar from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Al-Qummi also narrated it in his Tafsir as a mudmar and mursal narration.

In the same source, also from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): لا صلاة لمن لم تطع الصلاة وطاعة الصلاة أن تنتهي عن الفحشاء والمنكر — "There is no prayer for one who does not obey the prayer, and the obedience of the prayer is to cease from indecency and wrongdoing."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mas‘ud and others.

In the same source, Anas narrated: "A youth from the Ansar used to perform the prayers with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) yet commit immoralities. This was described to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and he said: 'Indeed, his prayer will prevent him one day.'"

In the same source, our companions narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Whoever wishes to know whether his prayer has been accepted or not, let him look: has his prayer prevented him from indecency and wrongdoing? To the extent that it prevented him, his prayer has been accepted."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَذِكْرُ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ — and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater (29:45), in a narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): "Regarding His saying: وَلَذِكْرُ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ, he says: 'Allah’s remembrance of the people of prayer is greater than their remembrance of Him—do you not see that He says: فَاذْكُرُونِي أَذْكُرْكُمْ (So remember Me, and I will remember you)?'"

I say: This is one of the meanings whose report was previously mentioned.

In Nur al-Thaqalayn, from Majma‘ al-Bayan, our companions narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The remembrance of Allah (ذكر الله) is at the time of what He has made lawful and what He has made prohibited."

In the same source, from Mu‘adh ibn Jabal, who said: "I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): 'Which deeds are most beloved to Allah?' He said: 'That you die while your tongue is moist with the remembrance of Allah (ذكر الله), the Mighty and Majestic.'"

In the same source, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "O Mu‘adh, indeed the foremost (السابقون) are those who stay awake with the remembrance of Allah (ذكر الله), the Mighty and Majestic. And whoever wishes to graze in the gardens of Paradise, let him increase in the remembrance of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from al-‘Abdi from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "Regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: بَلْ هُوَ آياتٌ بَيِّناتٌ فِي صُدُورِ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ (Rather it is [a set of] clear signs in the breasts of those who have been given knowledge), he said: 'They are the Imams.'"

I say: This meaning is narrated in al-Kafi and in Basa’ir al-Darajat through several paths. It is of the nature of application (الجري), meaning the application of the verse to the most perfect of instances, as evidenced by the following narration.

In al-Basa’ir, with its chain of transmission from Burayd ibn Mu‘awiyah from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "I said to him: بَلْ هُوَ آياتٌ بَيِّناتٌ فِي صُدُورِ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ, and he said: 'You are they; who else could they be?'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Isma‘ili in his Mu‘jam and Ibn Mardawayh extracted through the path of Yahya ibn Ja‘dah from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "Some people from the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to write from the Torah. They mentioned that to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and he said: 'Indeed, the most foolish of folly and the most straying of misguidance is a people who desire—away from what their Prophet brought—to a prophet other than their Prophet, and to a nation other than their nation.' Then Allah revealed: أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِهِمْ أَنَّا أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ يُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ (Does it not suffice them that We have sent down to you the Book which is recited to them?)."

In the same source, Ibn ‘Asakir extracted from Ibn Abi Mulaykah, who said: "‘Abdullah ibn ‘Amir ibn Kurayz sent a gift to ‘A’ishah. She thought it was from ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Umar, so she returned it and said: 'He follows the books, while Allah has said: أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِهِمْ أَنَّا أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ يُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ.' It was said to her: 'It is from ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Amir,' so she accepted it."

I say: The outward sense of the two narrations, especially the first, is that the verse was revealed regarding some of the companions, but the context of the verses rejects that.

Ayahs

يَـٰعِبَادِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ أَرْضِى وَٰسِعَةٌ فَإِيَّـٰىَ فَٱعْبُدُونِ (٥٦)
O My servants who have faith! My earth is indeed vast. So worship [only] Me. (56)
