كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَآئِقَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَيْنَا تُرْجَعُونَ (٥٧)
Every soul shall taste death. Then you shall be brought back to Us. (57)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُم مِّنَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ غُرَفًا تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ نِعْمَ أَجْرُ ٱلْعَـٰمِلِينَ (٥٨)
Those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We will surely settle them in the lofty abodes of paradise, with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever]. How excellent is the reward of the workers! (58)
ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ (٥٩)
—Those who are patient and who put their trust in their Lord. (59)
وَكَأَيِّن مِّن دَآبَّةٍ لَّا تَحْمِلُ رِزْقَهَا ٱللَّهُ يَرْزُقُهَا وَإِيَّاكُمْ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٦٠)
How many an animal there is that does not carry its own provision. Allah provides them and you, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (60)

(Commentary)

When the discourse concluded its rebuke of those believers who apostatized from their religion out of fear of persecution (الفتنة), it turned its attention toward the remaining believers—those whom the polytheists in Makkah had deemed weak and were threatening with persecution and torment. The verses command them to be patient, to put their trust in their Lord, and to emigrate from that land if their religious affairs and the performance of their obligatory duties (فرائضه) become difficult. They are told not to fear for their provision (الرزق), for provision is the responsibility of Allah, the Glorified; He will provide for them if they depart and emigrate just as He provided for them in their place of residence.

His saying, the Exalted: يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّ أَرْضِي واسِعَةٌ فَإِيَّايَ فَاعْبُدُونِ "O My servants who have faith! My earth is indeed vast. So Me [alone] let you worship." (29:56). This is a direction of speech toward the believers who were situated in the land of disbelief (أرض الكفر), unable to openly manifest the true religion or practice its traditions (الاستنان بسنته). The conclusion of the verse indicates this.

His saying: إِنَّ أَرْضِي واسِعَةٌ "My earth is indeed vast" — what appears from the context is that "the earth" refers to this physical earth upon which we live. Its attribution to the first-person pronoun is to indicate that the entire earth is the same to Him; it matters not in which portion of it He is worshipped. The vastness of the earth is a metaphor (كناية) for the fact that if it is impossible to adopt the true religion and act upon it in one region, there are other regions where this is not impossible. Thus, worshipping Him, the Exalted, alone is not impossible under any circumstances.

His saying: فَإِيَّايَ فَاعْبُدُونِ "So Me [alone] let you worship" — the first fa (الفاء) indicates a derivation (التفريع) from the vastness of the earth; meaning, if such is the case, then worship Me alone. The second fa is the fa of recompense (فاء الجزاء) for a deleted conditional clause implied by the speech. It appears that the advancement of the object "Me" (إياي) is to signify exclusivity (الحصر), making it a restriction of reversal (قصر قلب). The meaning is: do not worship other than Me, but rather worship Me. His saying: فَاعْبُدُونِ "let you worship" stands in the place of the recompense.

The summary of the meaning is: My earth is vast; if worshipping Me is impossible for you in one region of it, another region is spacious enough for My worship. If that is the case, then worship Me alone and do not worship anyone else. If you cannot worship Me in one piece of land, then emigrate to another and worship Me alone therein.

His saying, the Exalted: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ ثُمَّ إِلَيْنا تُرْجَعُونَ "Every soul shall taste death. Then you shall be brought back to Us." (29:57). This verse is an emphasis of the previous command in His saying: "So Me [alone] let you worship," and serves as a preamble (التوطئة) for the upcoming saying: "those who are patient," etc.

His saying: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ "Every soul shall taste death" is a form of metaphorical allusion (استعارة بالكناية), meaning that every soul will inevitably die. The shift (الالتفات) in His saying: ثُمَّ إِلَيْنا تُرْجَعُونَ "Then you shall be brought back to Us" from the context of the singular speaker to the context of the speaker with others is to indicate majesty (العظمة).

The summary of the meaning is: the life of this world is but a few days, and death lies behind it, followed by the return to Us for the reckoning. Therefore, let not the adornment of the worldly life—which is a vanishing adornment—hinder you from preparing for the meeting with Allah through faith and righteous deeds. For in that lies eternal happiness, while deprivation from it leads to everlasting and perpetual perdition.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ غُرَفاً "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We shall surely settle them in the lofts of paradise..." (29:58) etc. This is an exposition of the reward for faith and righteous deeds (الصالحات) — those deeds that are inherently good and performed in accordance with divine command — after death and the return to Allah. It contains an encouragement and inducement for the believers to maintain patience (الصبر) for the sake of Allah and to put their trust (التوكل) in Allah. Settling (التبوئة) means lodging someone for the purpose of permanent residence. Lofts (الغرف) is the plural of loft (غرفة), which refers to the upper room (العلية) in a house.

The Exalted first explained the reward of those who believe and perform righteous deeds, then He named them "workers" (عاملين) when He said: نِعْمَ أَجْرُ الْعامِلِينَ "How excellent is the reward of the workers!" (29:58). He then interpreted the "workers" with His saying: الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَعَلى رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ "those who are patient and put their trust in their Lord" (29:59). Thus, patience and trust became a specific mark (سمة) of the believers. All of this indicates that a believer is only truly satisfied with his faith when he is patient for the sake of Allah and relies upon Him. Therefore, a believer must be patient for Allah's sake against every harm and mistreatment as long as he finds a way to live a religious life. If the performance of religious rites becomes impossible in his land, he should depart and emigrate to another land, being patient with whatever exhaustion and hardship he encounters for the sake of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَعَلى رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ "those who are patient and put their trust in their Lord" (29:59) is a description of the workers. Patience (الصبر) — the steadfast endurance and self-restraint in the face of difficulty or temptation — is more general than patience during calamity, patience in obedience, or patience against disobedience. Although the context here is patience during calamity, which is appropriate for the state of the believers in Makkah who were commanded to emigrate.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ دَابَّةٍ لا تَحْمِلُ رِزْقَهَا اللهُ يَرْزُقُها وَإِيَّاكُمْ وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ "How many a creature there is that does not carry its own provision! Allah provides for it and for you, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing." (29:60). The term ka-ayyin (وكأين) is used for multiplicity. Carrying provision (حمل الرزق) refers to storing it, as is done by humans, ants, mice, and bees among other animals.

In the verse, there is a comforting of the believers' souls and a strengthening of their hearts, assuring them that if they emigrate for the sake of Allah, their provision will reach them wherever they are. They will not die of hunger, for their provider is their Lord, not their homelands. It says: many a creature has no stored provision, yet Allah provides for it and for you, O assembly of humans who store provisions; and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing.

The conclusion of the verse with the two Noble Names, the All-Hearing (السميع) and the All-Knowing (العليم), points to the proof for its content: that man and all other creatures are in need of provision, asking Allah for it by the tongue of their need. Allah, the Glorified, is the Hearer of supplication and the Knower of the needs of His creation, and the requirement of these two Noble Names is that He provides for them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, and in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّ أَرْضِي واسِعَةٌ "O My servants who have faith! My earth is indeed vast," he said: "It means: do not obey the people of wickedness among the kings. If you fear that they will persecute you regarding your religion, then My earth is vast." And He says: فِيمَ كُنْتُمْ قالُوا كُنَّا مُسْتَضْعَفِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ "‘In what state were you?’ They will say, ‘We were abased in the land.’" (4:97), so He said: أَلَمْ تَكُنْ أَرْضُ اللهِ واسِعَةً فَتُهاجِرُوا فِيها "‘Was not Allah’s earth vast so that you might have migrated in it?’" (4:97).

In al-Majma', Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Its meaning is: if Allah is disobeyed in a land where you reside, then depart from it to another."

In al-Uyun, with its chain of transmission to Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him), he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "When إِنَّكَ مَيِّتٌ وَإِنَّهُمْ مَيِّتُونَ 'You will surely die, and they [too] will surely die' (39:30) was revealed, I said: 'O Lord, will all of creation die while the Prophets remain?' Then كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ 'Every soul shall taste death' (29:57) was revealed."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mardawayh from 'Ali. However, its text is not without issues, for His saying: "You will surely die, and they [too] will surely die" informs of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) death and the death of all other people. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) knew that the Prophets who preceded him had died, so there is no meaning to his saying: "Will all of creation die while the Prophets remain?"

In al-Majma', from 'Ata from Ibn 'Umar, who said: "We went out with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) until we entered one of the gardens of the Ansar. He began picking dates and eating, and he said to me: 'O Ibn 'Umar, why do you not eat?' I said: 'I have no appetite for it, O Messenger of Allah.' He said: 'But I have an appetite for it, and this is the fourth morning since I have tasted food. Had I wished, I would have called upon my Lord and He would have given me the like of the kingdom of Khosrow and Caesar. How will it be with you, O Ibn 'Umar, when you remain among a people who hide away the provision of their year due to the weakness of their certainty?' By Allah, we did not leave until وَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ دَابَّةٍ لا تَحْمِلُ رِزْقَهَا اللهُ يَرْزُقُها وَإِيَّاكُمْ ـ وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ 'How many a creature there is that does not carry its own provision! Allah provides for it and for you, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing' (29:60) was revealed."

I say: This narration was also recorded in al-Durr al-Manthur, but its chain of transmission was deemed weak. Furthermore, it does not harmonize with the occurrence of the verse within the context of what preceded it.

Ayahs

وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَسَخَّرَ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ لَيَقُولُنَّ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ (٦١)
If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth, and who has disposed the sun and the moon?’ They will surely say, ‘Allah.’ Then where do they stray? (61)
ٱللَّهُ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَقْدِرُ لَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٦٢)
Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes of His servants, and tightens it for him. Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things. (62)
وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّن نَّزَّلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَوْتِهَا لَيَقُولُنَّ ٱللَّهُ ۚ قُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٦٣)
And if you ask them, ‘Who sends down water from the sky, with which He revives the earth after its death?’ They will surely say, ‘Allah.’ Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah!’ But most of them do not exercise their reason. (63)
وَمَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلدَّارَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ لَهِىَ ٱلْحَيَوَانُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (٦٤)
The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life (itself), had they known! (64)
فَإِذَا رَكِبُوا۟ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ دَعَوُا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ فَلَمَّا نَجَّىٰهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْبَرِّ إِذَا هُمْ يُشْرِكُونَ (٦٥)
When they board the ship, they invoke Allah putting exclusive faith in Him, but when He delivers them to land, behold, they ascribe partners [to Him], (65)
لِيَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ وَلِيَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (٦٦)
being ungrateful for what We have given them! Let them enjoy. Soon they will know! (66)
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّا جَعَلْنَا حَرَمًا ءَامِنًا وَيُتَخَطَّفُ ٱلنَّاسُ مِنْ حَوْلِهِمْ ۚ أَفَبِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ يُؤْمِنُونَ وَبِنِعْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ يَكْفُرُونَ (٦٧)
Have they not seen that We have appointed a safe sanctuary, while the people are despoiled all around them? Would they then believe in falsehood and be ungrateful toward the blessing of Allah? (67)
وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُۥٓ ۚ أَلَيْسَ فِى جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوًى لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٦٨)
Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or denies the truth when it comes to him? Is not the [final] abode of the faithless in hell? (68)
وَٱلَّذِينَ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِينَا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنَا ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَمَعَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٦٩)
As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous. (69)

(Commentary)

The verses shift the address from the believers to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). In meaning, it is a general address encompassing everyone, even if in wording it is specific to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), because the arguments mentioned therein are accessible to all.

The verses mention contradictions in the views of the polytheists regarding what was presented to the believers in the previous section—which they believed in. For they admit that the Creator of the heavens and the earth and the Disposer of the sun and the moon—upon which the cycles of sustenance revolve—is Allah. They admit that the One who sends down water from the sky and gives life to the earth after its death is Allah, the Glorified; yet they call upon others to provide for them. They worship Him, the Exalted, when they board ships at sea, but when He delivers them, they worship others. They reside in a secure sanctuary (حرم آمن), which is a blessing for them, yet they believe in falsehood, deny the truth, and are ungrateful for the blessing of Allah.

The conclusion of the Surah with His saying: وَالَّذِينَ جَاهَدُوا فِينَا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنَا "As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them to Our ways" (29:69) accords with the opening of the Surah: أَحَسِبَ النَّاسُ أَنْ يُتْرَكُوا أَنْ يَقُولُوا آمَنَّا وَهُمْ لَا يُفْتَنُونَ "Do the people suppose that they will be let off because they say, ‘We believe,’ and they will not be tested?" (29:2) until His saying: وَمَنْ جَاهَدَ فَإِنَّمَا يُجَاهِدُ لِنَفْسِهِ "Whoever strives, strives only for his own sake" (29:6), etc.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَسَخَّرَ الشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ فَأَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth, and disposed the sun and the moon?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah.’ Then whereunto are they turned away? (29:61).

The creation of the heavens and the earth pertains to origination (الإيجاد), while the disposal of the sun and the moon—by alternating their states through rising and setting, and their proximity to and distance from the earth—pertains to governance (التدبير), from which branches the formation of sustenance for humans and other animals. This creation and governance are inseparable; whoever admits to one must admit to the other.

Since Allah is the Creator and in His hand is the governance of the heavens, followed by the governance of the earth and the formation of sustenance, He is the One who must be called upon for provision and all other governance. It is therefore a wonder that a person is turned away from Him toward others who possess nothing. This is His saying: فَأَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ "Then whereunto are they turned away?" meaning: if the creation and the disposal of the sun and the moon belong to Him, the Exalted, how are these people turned away toward calling upon and worshipping idols?

His saying, the Exalted: اللهُ يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ وَيَقْدِرُ لَهُ إِنَّ اللهَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes of His servants, and straitens it for him. Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things. (29:62). This verse explicitly states what the previous verse alluded to. "Straitening" (القدر) is the opposite of "expanding" (البسط), which refers to its necessary meaning, namely "enlarging" (التوسعة). The use of the manifest noun in place of the pronoun in His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ "Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things" is to indicate the justification for the ruling. The meaning is: He has knowledge of all things because He is Allah.

The meaning is: Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes of His servants and restricts it for whomever He wishes—and He only wishes according to what is beneficial (المصلحة)—because He has knowledge of all things, for He is Allah, the Essence (الذات) that encompasses all attributes of perfection.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ نَزَّلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَحْيا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ مِنْ بَعْدِ مَوْتِها — until His saying — لا يَعْقِلُونَ And if you ask them, ‘Who sends down water from the sky, with which He revives the earth after its death?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah.’ Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah!’ But most of them do not exercise their reason. (29:63). What is meant by reviving the earth after its death is the growing of vegetation in the spring.

His saying: قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ "Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah!’" means: praise Allah for the completion of the proof against them through their admission that Allah is the Disposer of the affairs of His creation; thus, it becomes incumbent upon them to worship Him alone, excluding idols and the lords of the idols.

His saying: بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْقِلُونَ "But most of them do not exercise their reason" means they do not contemplate the signs nor do they let their intellects (العقول) judge so that they might know Allah and distinguish truth from falsehood; thus, they do not truly exercise their reason.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوانُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life (itself), had they known! (29:64). Diversion (اللهو) is that which diverts and distracts you from what is important to you. The life of this world is a diversion because it diverts the human being and distracts him with its transient, embellished adornments from the eternal, everlasting life.

Play (اللعب) is an act or a series of acts organized with an imaginary order for an imaginary goal, like the games of children. The life of this world is play because it is fleeting and quickly vanishes, like the play of children who gather for it, become engrossed in it for an hour, and then disperse—and how quickly they disperse.

Furthermore, the general objectives for which competitors compete and for which the unjust struggle are illusory matters like a mirage, such as wealth, spouses, children, types of advancement, prominence, leadership, mastery, servants, supporters, and others. A person possesses none of these except within the realm of illusion and imagination (الوهم والخيال).

As for the life of the Hereafter, in which the human being lives with his actual perfection (بكماله الواقعي) acquired through his faith and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), it is the "important matter" in which there is no diversion, and the "seriousness" in which there is no play, nor vain talk, nor sin. It is the "permanence" with which there is no annihilation, the "pleasure" accompanied by no pain, and the "felicity" followed by no misery. It is life in the true sense of the word.

This is the meaning of His saying, the Glorified: وَما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوانُ "The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life (itself)."

In the verse—as you see—there is a restriction (قصر) of the worldly life to diversion and play, and the use of the demonstrative "this" (هذه) to imply contempt (التحقير). There is also a restriction of the life of the Hereafter to "Life" (الحيوان) — the true and essential life — emphasized with tools of emphasis such as "indeed" (إن), the "lam" (اللام), the separating pronoun (ضمير الفصل), and the nominal sentence structure.

His saying: لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ "had they known!" means: if they had knowledge, they would have known that the matter is as We have described.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا رَكِبُوا فِي الْفُلْكِ دَعَوُا اللهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ فَلَمَّا نَجَّاهُمْ إِلَى الْبَرِّ إِذا هُمْ يُشْرِكُونَ When they board the ship, they invoke Allah putting exclusive faith in Him, but when He delivers them to land, behold, they ascribe partners [to Him] (29:65). This is a corollary to what was established in the previous verses regarding their state—that they are turned away and that many of them do not exercise their reason. That is, since they were turned away from His worship to the worship of others, and most of them do not reason and contradict themselves through admission and denial, "when they board," etc.

"Boarding" (الركوب) is to be elevated by sitting upon a moving object. It is a transitive verb, and its being made transitive with "in" (في) in the verse is because it implies the meaning of "settling" (الاستقرار) or something similar. The meaning is: when they board, settling in the ship, or they settle in the ship while boarding. The meaning of the verse is clear, recounting another contradiction of theirs and their ingratitude for the blessing.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيَكْفُرُوا بِما آتَيْناهُمْ وَلِيَتَمَتَّعُوا فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ Let them be ungrateful for what We have given them, and let them enjoy themselves; soon they will know! (29:66). The "lam" in لِيَكْفُرُوا "let them be ungrateful" and لِيَتَمَتَّعُوا "let them enjoy" is the "lam" of command (لام الأمر). A command from a commander for something he does not approve of is a threat and a warning, like saying to someone you are threatening: "Do what you wish." The Exalted said: اعْمَلُوا ما شِئْتُمْ إِنَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ "Do whatever you wish; indeed He watches what you do" (41:40).

It is also possible that the "lam" is for the "consequence" (للغاية), meaning: they perform these deeds so that they end up in ingratitude for the blessing We gave them and in [fleeting] enjoyment. The first of the two views is more in accord with His saying at the end of the verse: فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ "soon they will know!" This is supported by His saying in another place: لِيَكْفُرُوا بِما آتَيْناهُمْ فَتَمَتَّعُوا فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ "to be ungrateful for what We have given them. So enjoy yourselves; soon you will know!" (30:34). For this reason, those who read وَلِيَتَمَتَّعُوا with a silent "lam" did so because only the "lam" of command is made silent.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا جَعَلْنا حَرَماً آمِناً وَيُتَخَطَّفُ النَّاسُ مِنْ حَوْلِهِمْ Have they not seen that We have appointed a secure sanctuary, while the people are despoiled all around them? (29:67). The secure sanctuary (الحرم الآمن) is Mecca and its surroundings, which Allah made a place of safety through the prayer of Ibrahim (عليه السلام). "Despoiling" (التخطف) is like snatching (الخطف)—the seizing of something with speed and stealth. The Arabs at that time lived in a state of raiding and plundering, constantly attacking one another with killing, captivity, and looting; however, they respected the Sanctuary and would not harm anyone residing within it.

The meaning is: Have they not looked at how We made a secure sanctuary where no one is subjected to killing, captivity, or looting, while people are being snatched away all around them outside the Sanctuary?

His saying: أَفَبِالْباطِلِ يُؤْمِنُونَ وَبِنِعْمَةِ اللهِ يَكْفُرُونَ Do they then believe in falsehood and deny the blessing of Allah? (29:67) is another rebuke for them, as they meet this blessing—which is a great blessing—with ingratitude, yet they believe in idols, which are falsehood (باطل) having nothing but a name.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِالْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَهُ أَلَيْسَ فِي جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوىً لِلْكافِرِينَ Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or denies the truth when it comes to him? Is not the abode of the faithless in hell? (29:68). This is a threat to them with the Fire by branding them with the most severe and greatest wrongdoing (أشد الظلم), which is fabricating a lie against Allah by speaking of deities and that Allah has taken them as partners for Himself, and a person's denial of the truth when it comes to him. Both descriptions are present in them; they worshipped idols and denied the Quran when it came to them. Thus, they are disbelievers, and the "abode" (مثوى) and place of residence for the disbelievers in the Hereafter is Hell.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا وَإِنَّ اللهَ لَمَعَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them to Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous. (29:69). Striving (الجهد) is one's capacity and power, and struggling (المجاهدة) is the exhaustion of one's capacity in repelling the enemy. Jihad is of three types: struggling against the manifest enemy, struggling against Satan, and struggling against the self (النفس)—as mentioned by al-Raghib.

His saying: جاهَدُوا فِينا "strive in Us" means their striving is established "in Us," which is a metaphorical allusion (استعارة كنائية) to their effort being expended in what pertains to Him, the Exalted, in terms of belief and action. Thus, they are not turned away from faith in Him, nor from complying with His commands and refraining from His prohibitions, by any distractor that might turn them away.

His saying: لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا "We shall surely guide them to Our ways"—He attributed "ways" (سبل) to Himself, and these, whatever they may be, lead to Him, the Exalted. For a way is only a way because it leads to the one to whom the way belongs, who is its goal. So His ways are the paths that bring one near to Him and guide toward Him, the Exalted. Since the act of striving itself is a form of guidance, the guidance to the "ways" is guidance upon guidance; thus, it applies to such verses as His saying: وَالَّذِينَ اهْتَدَوْا زادَهُمْ هُدىً "As for those who are [rightly] guided, He enhances their guidance" (47:17).

From what has preceded, it appears there is no need in His saying فِينا "in Us" to assume a suppressed word like "affair" (شأن), as in "in Our affair."

His saying: وَإِنَّ اللهَ لَمَعَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ "and Allah is indeed with the virtuous"—it is said this means the "withness" (معية) of support and help, and the preceding mention of striving, which requires both, is a strong context for this intended meaning. [End quote]. This is a good view, but better than it is to interpret it as the "withness" of mercy and care (العناية), thus encompassing the "withness" of support, help, and other types of care that He, the Glorified, has for the virtuous among His servants due to the perfection of His care for them and the encompassment of His mercy over them. This "withness" is more specific than the "withness" of existence (معية الوجود) indicated by His saying: وَهُوَ مَعَكُمْ أَيْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ "and He is with you wherever you may be" (57:4).

It was previously noted that the verse is the conclusion of the Surah, turning back to its opening.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Abi al-Dunya and al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman narrated from Abu Ja'far that he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "How wondrous, how utterly wondrous is the one who believes in the Abode of Life (دار الحيوان) yet strives for the Abode of Delusion (دار الغرور)."

And in it: Juwaybir narrated from al-Dahhak from Ibn Abbas that they said: "O Muhammad, nothing prevents us from entering your religion except the fear that people will despoil us because of our small number, while the Arabs are more numerous than us. Whenever they hear that we have entered your religion, they will snatch us away—we would be but a single meal." So Allah revealed: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا جَعَلْنا حَرَماً آمِناً "Have they not seen that We have appointed a secure sanctuary..." the verse.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا وَإِنَّ اللهَ لَمَعَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ — in a narration by Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), he said: "This verse is for the Family of Muhammad (عليه السلام) and for their followers (أشياعهم)."
